Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker distorts the position of those who oppose the amendment (i.e., those who think otherwise) by attributing to them an extreme and unwarranted motive (hating America), rather than addressing any possible reasonable arguments against the amendment.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: The speaker makes claim "We should pass a constitutional amendment making it illegal to burn the American flag." The speaker restates the opposing view (anyone who thinks otherwise) as "just hates America" (a distorted attribution). The speaker attacks this distorted version by implying it is unpatriotic. Therefore, the opposing view is dismissed.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that opponents of the amendment hate America does not follow from the premise that the amendment should be passed, because the premise does not provide any evidence about the motives of those who disagree; the speaker has replaced the actual opposing argument with an exaggerated, emotional attack.

source_article: "The Cardinals are the best football team because they're better than all the other teams."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The Cardinals are better than all the other teams. Conclusion: The Cardinals are the best football team.
Step 2 - Pattern: The conclusion restates the premise in slightly different words, offering no independent evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: The Cardinals are the best team because they are better than all other teams. "Better than all other teams" is just another way of saying "the best team."
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise merely repeats the conclusion, providing no external support or evidence.

source_article: If you're going to add that much salt to the soup, you might as well pour the whole box in!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Adding a small extra amount of salt to soup is similar in effect to adding the whole box of salt. Conclusion: Adding that much salt is equivalent to pouring the whole box in.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument exaggerates a small increase into an extreme action, falsely assuming that because a little more salt is added, it is the same as adding all the salt, which is a hasty generalization from a moderate quantity to an extreme one.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (Hasty Generalization). Canonical form: [MSK1] (adding a small extra amount of salt) has attribute [MSK2] (being excessive). [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3] (adding any amount of salt). Therefore, all [MSK3] (adding the whole box) has attribute [MSK2].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because adding a moderate extra amount of salt is not logically equivalent to adding the entire box; the premise only describes a small increase, not an extreme one, so the generalization to the extreme case is unwarranted.

source_article: Senator Jones wants a strong national defense, but I will not vote for him because he moved to Canada to avoid being drafted for war when he was in college.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Senator Jones wants a strong national defense. Premise: He moved to Canada to avoid being drafted for war when he was in college. Conclusion: I will not vote for him.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the person (Senator Jones) based on an irrelevant past action (avoiding the draft) rather than addressing his position on national defense.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] avoided the draft. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Senator Jones is claiming we need strong national defense. He avoided the draft. Therefore, his claim about strong national defense should be dismissed.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because avoiding the draft decades ago does not logically refute or undermine Senator Jones’s current position on national defense; the attack is irrelevant to the argument.

source_article: “He’s not a great athlete; he’s a fraud, a cheat and a liar. That’s why not everybody is ‘happy for Lance.'”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The speaker claims Lance is a fraud, a cheat, and a liar. Conclusion: Therefore, not everybody is happy for Lance (implying his athletic achievements are invalid).
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks Lance’s character (calling him a fraud, cheat, liar) instead of addressing his athletic performance or specific actions that would justify the claim he is not a great athlete.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a moron. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: [The speaker] is claiming [Lance is not a great athlete]. [The speaker] calls Lance a fraud, cheat, liar. Therefore, Lance is not a great athlete.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Lance is not a great athlete does not follow from the premise that he is a fraud, cheat, and liar, because character flaws do not logically negate athletic ability or achievements.

source_article: My philosophy professor believes in ghosts and goes to séances. She’s an intelligent, educated, person, so ghosts must be real, and spiritualism must be true.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My philosophy professor believes in ghosts and goes to séances. She is intelligent and educated. Conclusion: Ghosts must be real, and spiritualism must be true.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the authority and credibility of a philosophy professor, implying that because she is intelligent and educated, her belief in ghosts and spiritualism makes those claims true. This is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] are intelligent and educated. Therefore, [MSK2] should be believed. Instantiation: [MSK1] = my philosophy professor, [MSK2] = ghosts are real and spiritualism is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because being intelligent and educated does not make one an expert on the supernatural; expertise in philosophy does not validate claims about ghosts, and personal belief is not evidence for objective reality.

source_article:
Scientist: Evolution explains how animals developed, adapted and diversified over millions of years.
Opponent: If we evolved from monkeys, why are there still monkeys? And why don't we have three arms? Wouldn't that give me a competitive advantage?
What kind of a logical fallacy is this?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Scientist says "Evolution explains how animals developed, adapted and diversified over millions of years." Conclusion: Opponent attacks by asking "If we evolved from monkeys, why are there still monkeys? And why don't we have three arms? Wouldn't that give me a competitive advantage?"

Step 2 - Pattern: The Opponent misrepresents the Scientist's claim about evolution (which explains common ancestry and diversification over millions of years) as a simplistic claim that humans evolved directly from modern monkeys and that evolution should produce perfect adaptations like three arms, then attacks these distorted versions.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Scientist makes claim "Evolution explains how animals developed, adapted and diversified over millions of years." Opponent restates this as "we evolved from monkeys" and "evolution should give us three arms" (distorted versions). Opponent attacks these distorted versions with rhetorical questions. Therefore, the original claim about evolution is implied to be false.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the Scientist's claim about evolution is false does not follow from the premise, because the Opponent's questions are based on a distorted, simplistic view of evolution (direct descent from modern monkeys and expectation of perfect adaptations) rather than addressing the actual scientific theory of common ancestry and gradual change over millions of years.

source_article: Trump presents a dark image of America if Clinton is elected, with “Syrian refugees flooding in, illegal immigrants convicted of crimes staying.”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Trump claims that if Clinton is elected, Syrian refugees will flood in and illegal immigrants convicted of crimes will stay. Conclusion: Therefore, if Clinton is elected, America will become a dark, dangerous place.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes a few specific, negative scenarios (Syrian refugees and criminal illegal immigrants) and generalizes them to represent the entire outcome of a Clinton presidency, implying that these isolated cases define the whole.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Syrian refugees and criminal illegal immigrants [MSK1] have the attribute of causing a dark America [MSK2]. These are a subset of the outcomes of a Clinton presidency [MSK3]. Therefore, all outcomes of a Clinton presidency [MSK3] have the attribute of causing a dark America [MSK2].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only focuses on a few negative possibilities, ignoring other potential policies, economic factors, and positive outcomes, thus overgeneralizing from limited evidence.

source_article: "Blondes are less intelligent than people with other hair colors."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Some blondes are less intelligent than people with other hair colors (implied from a few instances). Conclusion: All blondes are less intelligent than people with other hair colors.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes a characteristic (lower intelligence) observed in a few individuals with a specific hair color (blonde) and generalizes it to the entire group of blondes, ignoring other factors and evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Some blondes have lower intelligence. Blondes are a subset of people with blonde hair. Therefore, all blondes have lower intelligence.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only refers to a few instances of blondes, but the conclusion makes a sweeping claim about all blondes, which is unsupported and likely false due to individual variation.

source_article: No one has ever been able to prove that extraterrestrials exist, so they must not be real.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: No one has ever been able to prove that extraterrestrials exist. Conclusion: Extraterrestrials must not be real.

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker uses a lack of proof (absence of evidence) as direct evidence for non-existence, showing an intent to win the argument by appealing to ignorance rather than providing positive evidence for non-existence.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows that "no one has proven existence" does not prove non-existence, yet still claims "they must not be real" using an appeal to ignorance.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (lack of proof) does not logically imply non-existence; the argument intentionally shifts the burden of proof and uses an absence of evidence as if it were evidence of absence.

source_article: The red bicycle is red.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: None explicitly stated. Conclusion: "The red bicycle is red."
Step 2 - Pattern: The statement asserts a tautology where the predicate ("is red") is identical to the subject's attribute ("red"), so the conclusion merely repeats the premise without any independent support.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: "The red bicycle is red" is true because the bicycle is red, and the bicycle is red because it is a red bicycle.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from any premise because it is a self-referential statement that provides no new information or evidence; it simply restates the same claim in different words, failing to prove anything.

source_article: “I don’t understand why you’re saying I broke a promise. I said I’d never speak again to my ex-girlfriend. And I didn’t. I just sent her a quick text.”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I said I’d never speak again to my ex-girlfriend, and I didn’t speak to her. Conclusion: I didn’t break a promise.
Step 2 - Pattern: The term “speak” in the premise refers to verbal communication, but in the conclusion it is reinterpreted to exclude text messaging, shifting from a broad meaning of any spoken words to a narrow meaning of only oral conversation.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: “speak” is used to mean communicate verbally in the premise. “speak” is used to mean only oral, not written, communication in the conclusion.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that no spoken words were exchanged does not imply that no communication occurred; the meaning of “speak” shifts from verbal communication to a narrower sense excluding texts, so the promise not to speak could reasonably include any form of communication.

source_article: My job interview did not go well. I'll never get a job.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My job interview did not go well. Conclusion: I'll never get a job.
Step 2 - Pattern: A single negative experience (one job interview) is used to conclude a universal negative outcome (never getting any job), ignoring the possibility of other interviews or opportunities.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [This one job interview] has attribute [did not go well]. [This one job interview] is a subset of [all job interviews]. Therefore, all [job interviews] have attribute [did not go well], implying one will never get a job.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because a single failed interview does not logically imply that all future interviews will also fail or that no job will ever be obtained.

source_article: Why does the law state that you have to be 21 years old to drink?  Does it really make any difference if you are 20 years and 364 days old?  That is absurd.  Therefore, if a single day makes no difference, then a collection of 1095 single days won’t make any difference. Therefore, changing the drinking age to 18 will not make any difference.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If a single day makes no difference (between 20 years 364 days and 21 years), then a collection of 1095 single days won’t make any difference. Conclusion: Changing the drinking age to 18 will not make any difference.
Step 2 - Pattern: The term "makes no difference" is used in the premise to refer to the trivial, imperceptible difference of one day in age, but in the conclusion it is used to mean a significant, cumulative difference of three years in age, shifting from a negligible incremental change to a substantial aggregate change.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: "makes no difference" is used to mean no perceptible change from a single day in the premise. "makes no difference" is used to mean no effect from a three-year age difference in the conclusion.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that a single day makes no difference does not logically imply that many days combined make no difference; the meaning of "makes no difference" shifts from trivial incremental change to substantial cumulative change.

source_article: Senator Jones supports a strong national defense. If you do vote for him, our country will be invaded, and it will start World War III.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Senator Jones supports a strong national defense. Conclusion: If you vote for him, our country will be invaded and World War III will start.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attempts to persuade by instilling fear of a catastrophic outcome (invasion and World War III) rather than providing evidence linking the senator's policy to that outcome.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion (Appeal to Fear). Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: The claim that voting for Senator Jones will lead to invasion and World War III is made without evidence. In place of evidence, fear of war is used to convince the listener to not vote for him.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because supporting a strong national defense does not logically cause invasion and World War III; the argument uses fear-mongering instead of factual reasoning.

source_article: Your either for America or against her!!!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: None explicitly stated. Conclusion: You must be either for America or against her.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument presents only two mutually exclusive options—being for America or against her—ignoring the possibility of neutral, mixed, or conditional stances.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you are for America or you are against America.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because there are many possible positions toward a country (e.g., supporting some policies but not others, being indifferent, or being critical while still patriotic) that are not captured by the forced binary choice.

source_article:
President Bennett is an effective communicator, because he has a natural talent for speaking with people.
against
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: President Bennett has a natural talent for speaking with people. Conclusion: President Bennett is an effective communicator.
Step 2 - Pattern: The premise essentially restates the conclusion using different words, providing no independent evidence; the argument moves in a circle.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: "President Bennett is an effective communicator" is true because "he has a natural talent for speaking with people." "He has a natural talent for speaking with people" is true because "he is an effective communicator."
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that President Bennett is an effective communicator does not follow from the premise because the premise (having a natural talent for speaking with people) is not an independent reason; it is just a rephrasing of the conclusion, so no genuine support is provided.

source_article: I know three redheads who have terrible tempers, and since Annabel has red hair, I’ll bet she has a terrible temper too.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I know three redheads who have terrible tempers. Conclusion: Annabel, who has red hair, has a terrible temper.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument generalizes from a small, non-random sample of three redheads to all redheads, then applies that generalization to a specific individual.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (hasty generalization subtype). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: The three redheads [MSK1] have terrible tempers [MSK2]. These three redheads are a subset of all redheads [MSK3]. Therefore, all redheads have terrible tempers, including Annabel.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because three instances are insufficient to establish a universal pattern for all redheads, ignoring possible variation and alternative explanations.

source_article: You said that because an authority thinks something, it must therefore be true.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: An authority thinks something. Conclusion: That something must therefore be true.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument treats the opinion of an authority as an infallible guarantee of truth, ignoring the possibility that authorities can be wrong or that their expertise may not apply. This is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] is an authority. Therefore, [MSK2] must be true. Instantiation: [MSK1] = an authority, [MSK2] = something the authority thinks.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because authority does not guarantee truth; authorities can be mistaken, biased, or lack relevant expertise, and the claim requires independent evidence beyond mere authority.

source_article: "The building is closed. I saw 2 people standing outside the door."What is this type of logical fallacy?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I saw 2 people standing outside the door. Conclusion: The building is closed.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker concludes that the building is closed based solely on seeing two people outside, without considering other possible reasons for people standing outside (e.g., waiting for someone, taking a break, etc.). This is a hasty generalization from a small sample.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (hasty generalization). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: The two people standing outside have the attribute of being outside a closed building. These two people are a subset of all possible indicators of the building being closed. Therefore, the building is closed.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the presence of two people outside does not necessarily mean the building is closed; they could be there for other reasons, and the sample is too small to generalize about the building's status.

source_article: I don't think we need to talk to the people in the Justice Department about the practicality of enforcing this bill. The Justice Department is full of Democrats, and they are already biased against it.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The Justice Department is full of Democrats and they are biased against the bill. Conclusion: We do not need to talk to them about the practicality of enforcing it.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the political affiliation of the people in the Justice Department rather than addressing their arguments or expertise about the bill's enforcement.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a Democrat. Therefore, [MSK2] is not worth considering. Instantiation: The Justice Department is claiming that the bill's enforcement is impractical. The Justice Department is full of Democrats. Therefore, their claim is not worth considering.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the political affiliation of the Justice Department does not logically invalidate their expertise or arguments about the practicality of enforcing the bill; the attack is irrelevant to the claim.

source_article:
Speaker 1: Did you torture the prisoner?
Speaker 2: No, we just held him under water for a while, and then did a mock hanging.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Speaker 2 says "No" to the question "Did you torture the prisoner?" Conclusion: Speaker 2's actions (holding under water, mock hanging) are not torture.
Step 2 - Pattern: The term "torture" is used in the premise with a specific legal or moral meaning that the questioner assumes includes severe physical or psychological abuse, but in the conclusion Speaker 2 implicitly redefines "torture" to exclude the specific methods used, shifting from a broad definition to a narrow, self-serving one.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: "torture" is used to mean severe physical or psychological abuse (including waterboarding and mock executions) in the premise. "torture" is used to mean only certain extreme forms of abuse (excluding the described methods) in the conclusion.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that Speaker 2 denies torture relies on a narrow, redefined meaning of "torture," while the questioner's original meaning includes the described actions; the equivocation allows Speaker 2 to evade the accusation without addressing the substance.

source_article: If I eat this donut today, I'll probably do it again tomorrow and the next day, and then I'll never be able to eat healthy again.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If I eat this donut today, I'll do it again tomorrow and the next day. Conclusion: Therefore, I'll never be able to eat healthy again.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that a single instance of eating a donut will inevitably lead to a chain of repeated actions, and that this chain will permanently prevent healthy eating, without evidence that one donut necessarily leads to a permanent pattern.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically a Slippery Slope). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Eating a donut today (MSK1) has the attribute of leading to a permanent unhealthy pattern (MSK2). This action is a subset of all eating occasions (MSK3). Therefore, all eating occasions will lead to a permanent unhealthy pattern. The canonical form maps imperfectly; a more natural description: A single event is assumed to cause an extreme chain reaction without justification.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because there is no necessary causal link between one donut and a permanent loss of healthy eating; the argument exaggerates the consequence of a single action.

source_article: use of people's desire to be part of the group to convince them
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: People have a desire to be part of the group. Conclusion: They should be convinced by appealing to that desire.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument exploits the psychological desire for social belonging to persuade someone, rather than providing logical evidence for the claim itself.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: Many people want to be part of the group. Therefore, the group’s belief or action is correct.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the desire to belong does not make a claim true or an action right; truth and validity are independent of popularity.

source_article: We must have a poltergeist in the house. When the dog ran under the table, the vase just seemed to jump off the shelf all by itself.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: When the dog ran under the table, the vase just seemed to jump off the shelf all by itself. Conclusion: We must have a poltergeist in the house.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that an unexplained event (vase moving) must have a supernatural cause (poltergeist), ignoring natural explanations (e.g., the dog bumped the table, a draft, or a loose shelf).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Non sequitur. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true. Therefore, [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: The vase jumped off the shelf (unexplained event) is true. Therefore, we have a poltergeist in the house is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only describes an event that could have many natural causes; there is no logical connection linking the event to a poltergeist.

source_article: Which case is not a False Dichotomy?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The text asks "Which case is not a False Dichotomy?" Conclusion: This statement is an instance of the fallacy of relevance.

Step 2 - Pattern: The text is a question about identifying a non-example of a different fallacy (False Dichotomy), which is irrelevant to establishing or exemplifying the fallacy of relevance. It does not present an argument that diverts attention from a primary issue.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that asking about a False Dichotomy implies a fallacy of relevance, whereas the question about False Dichotomy is unrelated to the pattern of relevance fallacies.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the text exemplifies fallacy of relevance does not follow because the text is merely a question about another fallacy, not an argument that introduces an irrelevant point to distract from a main argument.

source_article: Have you accepted the fact that most environmental studies don’t support global warming?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The question "Have you accepted the fact that most environmental studies don’t support global warming?" is posed. Conclusion: The listener must accept that global warming is unsupported by most environmental studies.
Step 2 - Pattern: The question presupposes a disputed fact (that most environmental studies don't support global warming) without providing evidence, showing an intent to win the argument by loading the question with an unsubstantiated claim.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows that the claim "most environmental studies don't support global warming" is not established as fact. The speaker still presents it as a given fact using a loaded question as an incorrect argument.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise is a false presupposition; the question assumes acceptance of an unsupported claim, which does not constitute valid evidence for the claim itself.

source_article:
"America: Love it or leave it"
This is an example of which kind of logical fallacy?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: None explicit. Conclusion: You must either love America or leave it.
Step 2 - Pattern: The statement presents only two mutually exclusive options—love the country or leave it—ignoring other possibilities like criticizing it while staying, working for change, or being neutral.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either [love America] or [leave America] is the only acceptable choice.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because there are many legitimate options beyond the two presented, so the forced choice is invalid.

source_article: The accident was caused by the taxi parking in the street
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The accident was caused by the taxi parking in the street. Conclusion: The taxi parking caused the accident.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument asserts a causal link between the taxi parking in the street and the accident without providing evidence that the parking directly led to the accident, ignoring other possible causes like driver error, road conditions, or mechanical failure.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Taxi parking in the street] occurred, then [accident] occurred. Therefore, [taxi parking] caused [accident].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise merely states a sequence or assumption of causation without supporting evidence; the accident could have been due to other factors, and the taxi parking may have been a coincidence or a non-causal factor.

source_article: "Some tall people recently vandalized the park. Tall people are so irresponsible."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Some tall people recently vandalized the park. Conclusion: Tall people are so irresponsible.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes a specific instance of a few tall people committing vandalism and generalizes this negative attribute to all tall people.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [some tall people] have attribute [vandalized the park]. [some tall people] is a subset of [tall people]. Therefore, all [tall people] have attribute [irresponsible].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only describes a few tall people, not all tall people; it unjustly extends a negative trait from a small sample to an entire group.

source_article: "The mind is like a knife, cutting through difficult problems. But just as too much cutting dulls a knife, so too much education dulls the mind."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The mind is like a knife, cutting through difficult problems. Premise: Just as too much cutting dulls a knife, so too much education dulls the mind. Conclusion: Too much education dulls the mind.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument draws a false analogy between a physical knife and the mind, assuming that because both are described as "cutting," they share the property of being dulled by overuse, ignoring that the mind's capacity for learning is not analogous to a knife's physical wear.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is False Analogy. Canonical form: [MSK1] has property [MSK2] in context A, so [MSK1] must have property [MSK2] in context B. Instantiation: A knife cuts and becomes dull with overuse, so the mind (which "cuts" through problems) must also become dull with overuse (too much education).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the analogy between a knife and the mind is weak; the mind does not physically dull from learning but instead can grow sharper or more capable, so the premise does not support the conclusion.

source_article: Portraying one's opponent's position in a simplified manner so that it is easy to argue against.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The arguer portrays an opponent's position in a simplified manner. Conclusion: The opponent's position is easy to argue against.

Step 2 - Pattern: The fallacy occurs by replacing the opponent's actual position with a simplified, weaker version, making it easier to attack, rather than addressing the original, more nuanced claim.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Opponent makes claim (unspecified). Arguer restates it in a simplified manner (distorted). Arguer attacks the simplified version. Therefore, the original claim is false.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the opponent's position is easy to argue against does not follow from the premise, because simplifying a position does not refute the original claim—it merely creates a weaker target that may not accurately represent the opponent's actual argument.

source_article: Do you think that we should convict this criminal?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The question asks "Do you think that we should convict this criminal?" Conclusion: The listener is expected to answer based on a presumption that the person is a criminal and should be convicted.
Step 2 - Pattern: The question is loaded, assuming the person is a criminal and that conviction is the relevant action, showing an intent to win the argument by forcing a response that accepts the unstated premise without evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows the person's guilt is not yet established. The speaker still claims the person is a criminal using a loaded question as an incorrect argument.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (the question) assumes guilt without supporting evidence, attempting to shift the burden of proof onto the listener to justify a conviction.

source_article: Running the government is like running a business. You can’t keep running into debt and expect to be successful.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Running the government is like running a business. Conclusion: You can’t keep running into debt and expect to be successful.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because a business cannot succeed with debt, the same rule applies to government, ignoring fundamental differences between the two entities.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] is like [MSK2]. [MSK2] has attribute [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK1] has attribute [MSK3]. Instantiation: Running the government is like running a business. A business cannot succeed with debt. Therefore, the government cannot succeed with debt.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that government is like business is a false analogy; government can manage debt differently (e.g., through taxation, monetary policy) than a private business, so the generalization from business to government is unwarranted.

source_article: In 2011, Christian broadcaster, Harold Camping, (once again) predicted the end of the world via Jesus, and managed to get many Christians to join his alarmist campaign.  During this time, and especially after the Armageddon date had passed,  many Christian groups publicly declared that Camping is not a “true Christian”.  On a personal note, I think Camping was and is as much of a Christian as any other self-proclaimed Christian and religious/political/ethical beliefs aside, I give him credit for having the cojones to make a falsifiable claim about his religious beliefs.Example #2:
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Christian groups publicly declared that Harold Camping is not a “true Christian” after his failed prediction. Conclusion: Camping is not a “true Christian” based on his failed prediction.
Step 2 - Pattern: The Christian groups use a subjective redefinition of “true Christian” to exclude Camping after his prediction failed, showing an intent to win the argument by moving the goalposts rather than engaging with Camping’s actual beliefs or evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The Christian groups know that Camping’s failed prediction does not automatically make him not a Christian. They still claim he is not a “true Christian” using a No True Scotsman fallacy.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (public declaration based on a failed prediction) does not logically prove Camping is not a Christian; the argument intentionally uses a shifting definition to dismiss him.

source_article: "The government should not prohibit drugs. Otherwise the government should also ban alcohol or cigarettes. And then fatty food and junk food would have to be regulated too. The next thing you know, the government would force us to brush our teeth and do exercises everyday."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If the government prohibits drugs, it should also ban alcohol, cigarettes, fatty food, and eventually force us to brush teeth and exercise. Conclusion: Therefore, the government should not prohibit drugs.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that taking one step (prohibiting drugs) will inevitably lead to a chain of increasingly extreme steps (banning all unhealthy items and forcing personal habits), without evidence that such a chain would actually occur.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] leads to [MSK2]. [MSK2] leads to [MSK3]. Therefore, [MSK1] leads to [MSK3] without justification. Instantiation: Prohibiting drugs leads to banning alcohol and cigarettes. Banning alcohol and cigarettes leads to regulating fatty food and junk food. Regulating those leads to forcing tooth brushing and exercise. Therefore, prohibiting drugs leads to forcing tooth brushing and exercise.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise asserts a slippery slope of consequences that are not causally connected or inevitable; each step is an exaggerated leap unsupported by evidence or logical necessity.

source_article: "If my years as a Marine taught me anything, it's that caution is the best policy in this sort of situation."?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My years as a Marine taught me that caution is the best policy in this sort of situation. Conclusion: Caution is the best policy in this sort of situation.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the speaker's personal experience and authority as a Marine to support the claim, which is an appeal to authority (specifically personal authority), a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2] based on [MSK3] experience. Therefore, [MSK2] should be accepted. Instantiation: [MSK1] = the speaker, [MSK2] = caution is the best policy, [MSK3] = years as a Marine.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because military experience does not necessarily qualify someone as an expert on the best policy in this specific situation, and the premise merely asserts the lesson learned without providing independent evidence that caution is indeed optimal.

source_article: "Chick fil A is the greatest food chain in existence. Look at the crowds!"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: There are crowds at Chick-fil-A. Conclusion: Therefore, Chick-fil-A is the greatest food chain in existence.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses the fact that many people eat at Chick-fil-A (the crowds) as evidence that it is the greatest food chain, assuming popularity implies quality or superiority.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people eat at Chick-fil-A (crowds). Therefore, Chick-fil-A must be the greatest food chain.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the size of the crowd does not logically prove that the food chain is the greatest; popularity can be due to many factors unrelated to objective quality, such as location, marketing, or convenience.

source_article: She is a teacher in a public school, so any claims that she makes about the public school system are biased and untrue.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: She is a teacher in a public school. Conclusion: Any claims that she makes about the public school system are biased and untrue.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the person's identity (being a public school teacher) to dismiss her claims as biased and false, rather than addressing the content of the claims themselves.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a teacher in a public school. Therefore, [MSK2] is biased and untrue. Instantiation: She is claiming something about the public school system. She is a teacher in a public school. Therefore, her claims are biased and untrue.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because being a teacher does not automatically make one's claims biased or false; the argument ignores the actual evidence or reasoning behind the claims and instead attacks the person's role.

source_article: This fallacy distracts from the argument by pointing out hypocrisy in the opponent.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: This fallacy distracts from the argument by pointing out hypocrisy in the opponent. Conclusion: (implied) This fallacy is ad hominem.

Step 2 - Pattern: The text defines a fallacy by its characteristic of attacking the opponent’s hypocrisy rather than engaging with the argument itself, which matches the ad hominem subtype of appeal to hypocrisy.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a hypocrite. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: The fallacy described points out hypocrisy in the opponent, so it instantiates the canonical form where the attack is on the person’s hypocrisy rather than the argument.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the text merely describes a fallacy without stating that the attack on hypocrisy is irrelevant to the argument’s truth; the description itself is a definition, not an argument that commits the fallacy.

source_article: "You didn't do your homework, did you?" is an example of...
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The question "You didn't do your homework, did you?" is presented as an example. Conclusion: The question is an instance of the intentional fallacy.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker intends to win an argument by framing the question in a way that assumes the listener did not do their homework, thereby hiding the lack of evidence for that assumption and shifting the burden of proof onto the listener to deny it.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The questioner knows the listener may have done their homework. The questioner still claims the listener did not do it using an incorrect argument (a loaded question that assumes guilt).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (the existence of the question) does not prove the question is an intentional fallacy; the argument relies on the speaker's intent to mislead without providing evidence that the homework was actually undone.

source_article: "Shell was charged with misleading advertising in its Platformate advertisements. A Shell spokesman said: 'The same comment could be made about most good advertising of most products.'"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The same comment could be made about most good advertising of most products. Conclusion: Shell's Platformate advertisements are not misleading.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument suggests that because many other advertisements (most good advertising) share a similar characteristic, Shell's specific advertisement must be acceptable or not misleading, appealing to the popularity or commonness of the practice.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people (or many similar cases) do [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] is acceptable or true. Instantiation: Many good advertisements do what Shell's ad does. Therefore, Shell's ad is not misleading.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the prevalence of a practice does not determine its truth or ethical standing; misleading advertising remains misleading regardless of how common such tactics are.

source_article: Professor X holds that the use of non-invasive ventilation has no place in the management of ARDS; this, therefore, is the approach we should adopt.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Professor X holds that the use of non-invasive ventilation has no place in the management of ARDS. Conclusion: This, therefore, is the approach we should adopt.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument relies solely on the assertion of an authority figure (Professor X) without providing independent evidence or reasoning, which is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] is an expert in the field concerning [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] should be adopted. Instantiation: [MSK1] = Professor X, [MSK2] = non-invasive ventilation has no place in ARDS management.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the mere opinion of an authority, even an expert, does not guarantee its correctness; the argument fails to consider other expert opinions, evidence, or the possibility that Professor X’s view might be controversial or outdated.

source_article: Andanar is unfit for the position because he seems unqualified for it.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: He seems unqualified for the position. Conclusion: Andanar is unfit for the position.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses the same claim as both premise and conclusion, restating "unqualified" as "unfit" without providing any independent evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]; [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: Andanar is unfit because he seems unqualified; he seems unqualified because he is unfit.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise merely rephrases the conclusion, offering no new or independent justification for why Andanar is unfit.

source_article: Mr. Equalminded's idea for implementing affirmative action in the university's admission process is complete folly. He says he wants the student body to represent diversity in the community. But that's ridiculous. I suppose this means we'll have to throw out our academic standards while were at it. After all, if we're going to let students into our prestigious institution just because of their gender of the color of their skin, then what's going to happen to our reputation?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The speaker argues that Mr. Equalminded's idea for affirmative action will lead to throwing out academic standards and damaging the university's reputation. Conclusion: Therefore, Mr. Equalminded's idea is complete folly.

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker diverts attention from the actual proposal of affirmative action (representing diversity) by introducing an exaggerated, unrelated consequence about discarding academic standards and harming reputation, rather than addressing the merits of the diversity goal itself.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that implementing affirmative action for diversity implies throwing out academic standards, whereas the goal of diversity is unrelated to necessarily discarding academic standards.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Mr. Equalminded's idea is folly does not follow from the premise because the claim about losing academic standards is an exaggerated red herring that does not address the actual proposal of affirmative action; it shifts focus away from the core argument about diversity.

source_article:
Speaker 1: I think marijuana should be legalized. It would be better for the country if we didn’t have this drug war.
Speaker 2: Of course you think that. You’re a pothead.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Speaker 1 is a pothead. Conclusion: Therefore, Speaker 1's claim that marijuana should be legalized is not valid.
Step 2 - Pattern: Instead of addressing the argument about legalizing marijuana and the drug war, Speaker 2 attacks Speaker 1's personal identity as a "pothead" to dismiss the argument.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a pothead. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Speaker 1 is claiming that marijuana should be legalized. Speaker 1 is a pothead. Therefore, marijuana should not be legalized.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Speaker 1's claim is invalid does not follow from the premise that Speaker 1 is a pothead, because a person's identity or lifestyle does not determine the truth or falsity of their argument.

source_article: Why would you trust Paris Hilton to sell health food? She drinks and parties and has multiple partners.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Paris Hilton drinks, parties, and has multiple partners. Conclusion: Therefore, you should not trust her to sell health food.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks Paris Hilton's personal lifestyle and behavior rather than addressing the quality or validity of the health food she is selling.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] has a bad lifestyle. Therefore, [MSK2] is not trustworthy. Instantiation: Paris Hilton is claiming to sell health food. She drinks and parties and has multiple partners. Therefore, you should not trust her health food.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because Paris Hilton's personal life is irrelevant to the quality or safety of the health food product she is endorsing; the argument should address the product itself.

source_article: Texting has killed people’s ability to write in complete sentences with proper grammar.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Texting has killed people’s ability to write in complete sentences with proper grammar. Conclusion: Texting caused the loss of ability to write in complete sentences with proper grammar.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because texting is associated with poor grammar in some contexts, it directly causes the loss of writing ability, without considering other factors like educational changes or generational differences.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Texting] occurred, then [people’s ability to write properly declined]. Therefore, [texting] caused [the decline].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only states a correlation; the decline could be due to other causes such as reduced emphasis on formal writing in education, changing communication norms, or other technological influences, and mere association does not prove causation.

source_article: Tik Tok is popular among teens because so many of them use it.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: So many teens use Tik Tok. Conclusion: Tik Tok is popular among teens.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses the same fact (that many teens use Tik Tok) as both the evidence for and the definition of popularity, creating a loop where neither is independently established.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: Tik Tok is popular among teens because so many of them use it. So many teens use it because it is popular among teens.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Tik Tok is popular among teens does not follow from the premise that many teens use it, because the premise is essentially a restatement of the conclusion rather than an independent reason.

source_article: "To get better schools, we have to raise taxes. If we don't, we can't have better schools."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: To get better schools, we have to raise taxes. If we don't, we can't have better schools. Conclusion: Raising taxes is the only way to achieve better schools.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument presents only two options—raising taxes or not having better schools—ignoring other possibilities such as reallocating existing funds, improving efficiency, or seeking alternative funding sources.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either we raise taxes or we cannot have better schools.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise falsely limits the choices to two, overlooking other viable ways to improve schools without raising taxes.

source_article: MacDougal roots for a British football team. Clearly he's unfit to be a police chief in Ireland.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: MacDougal roots for a British football team. Conclusion: MacDougal is unfit to be a police chief in Ireland.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks MacDougal's personal preference (supporting a British team) rather than addressing his qualifications or actions relevant to being a police chief.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] roots for a British football team. Therefore, [MSK1] is unfit to be a police chief in Ireland. Instantiation: MacDougal roots for a British football team. Therefore, MacDougal is unfit to be a police chief in Ireland.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because rooting for a British football team has no logical bearing on one's fitness to serve as a police chief; the premise is irrelevant to the conclusion.

source_article: Karen is a thirty-something-year-old female who drives a mini-van, lives in the suburbs, and wears mom jeans. Is Karen more likely to be a woman or a mom?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Karen is a thirty-something-year-old female who drives a mini-van, lives in the suburbs, and wears mom jeans. Conclusion: Karen is more likely to be a mom than a woman.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes a single example (Karen) with stereotypical traits associated with motherhood and generalizes that these traits make "mom" a more likely category than "woman," ignoring that all mothers are women and that the traits are not exclusive to mothers.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attributes [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK1] or [MSK3] with [MSK2] are [MSK4]. Instantiation: Karen has attributes (mini-van, suburbs, mom jeans). Karen is a woman. Therefore, a woman with these attributes is more likely a mom.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise provides no evidence that the traits are exclusive to mothers or that being a mom is a distinct category from being a woman; the reasoning relies on a hasty generalization from a single, stereotyped case.

source_article: These test results are clearly wrong, and it must be either because the client was malingering or because I bungled the test administration. Taking another look at the test manual, I see now that I bungled the test administration. Therefore the client was not malingering.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The test results are clearly wrong, and it must be either because the client was malingering or because I bungled the test administration. Premise: I bungled the test administration. Conclusion: Therefore the client was not malingering.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument presents a false dilemma by asserting that only two mutually exclusive causes exist for the wrong test results, ignoring other possible causes (e.g., test design flaws, external factors, or other examiner errors).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either the client was malingering or I bungled the test administration is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not logically follow because the premise falsely restricts the options to two; even if one option (bungled administration) is true, it does not eliminate the possibility of other causes, nor does it prove the client was not malingering, as both could coexist or other causes could be at play.

source_article: Not only is paying a higher income tax a patriotic duty, it is also a sacred obligation.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Paying a higher income tax is a patriotic duty and a sacred obligation. Conclusion: Paying a higher income tax is something that should be done.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument asserts that paying a higher income tax is correct or necessary by appealing to the idea that it is widely considered a patriotic duty and sacred obligation, implying that many people believe this and therefore it must be true.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people believe paying a higher income tax is a patriotic duty and sacred obligation. Therefore, paying a higher income tax must be true (or correct).

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the popularity or widespread belief that something is a duty or obligation does not logically prove that it is true or should be done; the argument relies on social pressure rather than evidence or reasoning.

source_article: The mayor argued that we need to reduce funding for the animal shelter in order to balance the budget. Don't vote for someone who doesn't think the government should care about animals.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The mayor argued that we need to reduce funding for the animal shelter in order to balance the budget. Conclusion: The speaker says "Don't vote for someone who doesn't think the government should care about animals."

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker misrepresents the mayor's specific proposal (reducing funding for one shelter to balance the budget) as a general claim that the government should not care about animals, then attacks that extreme, distorted version.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: The mayor makes claim "we need to reduce funding for the animal shelter to balance the budget." The speaker restates this as "the mayor doesn't think the government should care about animals" (a distorted extreme). The speaker attacks the distorted version by urging not to vote for such a person. Therefore, the mayor's proposal is implied to be wrong.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the mayor does not care about animals does not follow from the premise, because reducing funding for one shelter to balance a budget does not equate to a wholesale disregard for animal welfare; the speaker has replaced the specific, reasoned argument with a weaker, exaggerated version.

source_article: We live in paradise, but we don't know it.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: We live in paradise. Conclusion: We don't know it (or we are unaware of it).
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument presents a contradiction or paradox: it asserts that we live in paradise, yet simultaneously claims we are ignorant of that fact, without providing any logical connection or reasoning to justify the conclusion from the premise.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Self Contradiction (or Internal Contradiction). Canonical form: [MSK1] is true and [MSK1] is false (or contradictory). Instantiation: [We live in paradise] is true and [we don't know that we live in paradise] is true, which implies a contradiction if knowing is inherent to living in paradise.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise does not logically entail the conclusion; the statement merely juxtaposes two ideas without a valid logical link, and if paradise inherently implies awareness, then the conclusion contradicts the premise.

source_article: "I can't trust that doctor's advice on how to raise my child. He has never been a mother."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The doctor has never been a mother. Conclusion: I can't trust that doctor's advice on how to raise my child.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the doctor's personal experience (not being a mother) instead of addressing the quality or validity of the medical advice itself.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] has never been a mother. Therefore, [MSK2] is not trustworthy. Instantiation: The doctor is claiming advice on raising a child. The doctor has never been a mother. Therefore, the doctor's advice is not trustworthy.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the doctor's lack of personal maternal experience does not logically invalidate their professional medical knowledge about child-rearing.

source_article: All people from Pinole are liars
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: There are people from Pinole. Conclusion: All people from Pinole are liars.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the character or origin of a group (people from Pinole) rather than addressing any specific claim or evidence about their truthfulness.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is from Pinole. [MSK1] is a liar. Therefore, any claim by [MSK1] is false. Instantiation: The text generalizes an attack against a group's honesty based solely on their geographic origin, without addressing any argument.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise only states origin, which is irrelevant to the truthfulness of individuals; no evidence or reasoning supports the blanket accusation.

source_article: "Either you are for us, or you're against us!"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: There are only two possible positions: being for us or being against us. Conclusion: You must choose one of these two positions.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker presents a binary choice, ignoring the possibility of neutrality, partial support, or other nuanced stances.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you are for us or you are against us.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because many intermediate positions exist (e.g., neutral, partially supportive, or indifferent), so the forced choice is unwarranted.

source_article: Broccoli has significantly less fat than the leading candy bar!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Broccoli has significantly less fat than the leading candy bar. Conclusion: (implied) Broccoli is a healthier food than the leading candy bar, or that all vegetables are low-fat compared to all candy bars.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text compares a single vegetable (broccoli) to a single candy bar, then implies a general conclusion about the healthiness of vegetables versus candy bars based on only one attribute (fat content) from this single comparison.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Broccoli has significantly less fat than the leading candy bar. Broccoli is a subset of vegetables. Therefore, all vegetables have significantly less fat than all candy bars.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only compares one specific vegetable to one specific candy bar; it does not justify a general claim about all vegetables versus all candy bars, nor does it account for other nutritional factors beyond fat content.

source_article: There is definitely a link between autism and the vaccines given to toddlers. I know this because a Wikipedia entry mentioned numerous studies that found this to be true.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: A Wikipedia entry mentioned numerous studies that found a link between autism and vaccines given to toddlers. Conclusion: There is definitely a link between autism and the vaccines given to toddlers.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the authority of Wikipedia as a source, treating its mention of studies as sufficient evidence without evaluating the credibility of the studies themselves or the consensus of scientific experts.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a source of information. Therefore, [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: [MSK1] = Wikipedia entry, [MSK2] = there is a link between autism and vaccines.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because Wikipedia is not an authoritative source for medical claims; it can contain inaccurate or outdated information, and the mere mention of studies does not confirm their validity or the scientific consensus, which overwhelmingly rejects such a link.

source_article: If I made a reference to a monk I would most likely be utilizing...
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If I made a reference to a monk I would most likely be utilizing... Conclusion: (implicitly, that the reference is credible or appropriate because it involves a monk).
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the presumed authority or credibility of a monk (a religious figure) to support the reference, without providing evidence that the monk's status makes the reference valid. This is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] is a monk. Therefore, referencing [MSK1] is credible. Instantiation: [MSK1] = a monk. The premise is that the speaker would use a monk reference, implying the monk's authority lends credibility.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because being a monk does not automatically make the reference epistemically sound; the monk's expertise or relevance to the context is not established, and the argument lacks justification for why the monk's status should be trusted.

source_article: Why should you feel guilty for seeking your own happiness when that's what everyone else is doing, too?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: That's what everyone else is doing, too. Conclusion: You should not feel guilty for seeking your own happiness.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument justifies a lack of guilt by appealing to the fact that many others are doing the same thing, implying that popular behavior validates the action.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people are seeking their own happiness. Therefore, seeking your own happiness is acceptable and guilt-free.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the number of people engaging in an action does not determine its moral or ethical correctness; guilt is a personal ethical consideration, not a popularity contest.

source_article:
Interviewer: "Your resume looks impressive but I need another reference."
Bill: "Jill can give me a good reference."
Interviewer: "Good. But how do I know that Jill is trustworthy?"
Bill: "Certainly. I can vouch for her."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Bill says Jill can give him a good reference. Conclusion: Jill is trustworthy because Bill vouches for her.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses each person to vouch for the other's trustworthiness, creating a loop where the support for each claim depends on the other claim.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is trustworthy because [MSK2] vouches for [MSK1]. [MSK2] is trustworthy because [MSK1] vouches for [MSK2]. Instantiation: Jill is trustworthy because Bill vouches for her. Bill is trustworthy because Jill vouches for him.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Jill is trustworthy does not follow because the only evidence for her trustworthiness is Bill's statement, and Bill's trustworthiness is itself only supported by Jill's statement, providing no independent verification.

source_article: So you are saying your car is worth $20k.  I think it is worth $1, so let’s just compromise and say it is worth $10k. (Assuming the car is worth $20k)
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The speaker says the car is worth $20k, then proposes a compromise value of $10k, assuming the car is actually worth $20k. Conclusion: The compromise value of $10k is a reasonable resolution.

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker introduces a false compromise between the actual value ($20k) and an arbitrary low value ($1) to divert attention from the fact that the car’s true worth is $20k, rather than addressing the validity of the initial $20k claim.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that proposing a compromise between $20k and $1 implies the car is worth $10k, whereas the arbitrary low value of $1 is unrelated to the actual worth of the car.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that $10k is a fair compromise does not follow from the premise because the initial claim of $20k is the only relevant fact, and the $1 counteroffer is a false distraction; the compromise is not logically justified.

source_article: Cardenas roots for a British football team. Clearly he’s unfit to be a chef at the new restaurant
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Cardenas roots for a British football team. Conclusion: Cardenas is unfit to be a chef at the new restaurant.  
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks Cardenas’s personal football preference instead of providing any evidence about his cooking skills or qualifications.  
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a moron. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Cardenas is claiming to be a chef. Cardenas roots for a British football team. Therefore, Cardenas is unfit to be a chef.  
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because rooting for a British football team is irrelevant to culinary ability; no logical connection is made between the premise and the conclusion.

source_article: If you do not believe in God, you will go to hell.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If you do not believe in God, you will go to hell. Conclusion: You should believe in God.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument threatens a negative emotional outcome (eternal suffering) without providing evidence that the premise is true, using fear to persuade.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: [Believing in God to avoid hell] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, fear of eternal punishment is used to convince the interlocutor that [believing in God to avoid hell] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that one should believe in God does not follow from the premise because the premise itself is an unsupported threat; no evidence is given that hell exists or that disbelief leads there, so the argument relies solely on emotional manipulation rather than logical reasoning.

source_article: Albert Einstein was extremely impressed with this theory.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Albert Einstein was extremely impressed with this theory. Conclusion: The theory is credible or should be believed.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the opinion of a highly respected authority figure (Einstein) as evidence for the theory's validity, even though the theory may not be within Einstein's field of expertise or the statement does not provide substantive reasoning.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] was impressed with [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a famous authority. Therefore, [MSK2] is credible. Instantiation: [MSK1] = Albert Einstein, [MSK2] = this theory.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because Einstein's personal impression, without evidence of his expertise in the specific field or the reasoning behind his impression, does not logically validate the theory; authority alone is insufficient to establish truth.

source_article: The newspaper editor homeschools his children. So, any claims he makes about the public school system are biased and untrue.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The newspaper editor homeschools his children. Conclusion: Any claims he makes about the public school system are biased and untrue.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the person (the editor’s choice to homeschool) instead of addressing the content or reasoning of his claims about the public school system.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] homeschools his children. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The newspaper editor (MSK1) claims something about the public school system (MSK2). He homeschools his children. Therefore, his claims are biased and untrue.
Step 4 - Verdict: The editor’s homeschooling does not logically entail that his claims about the public school system are false or biased; the premise is irrelevant to the truth or validity of his arguments.

source_article: The book "Investing for Dummies" really helped me understand my finances better. The book "Chess for Dummies" was written by the same author, was published by the same press, and costs about the same amount, so it would probably help me understand my finances as well.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The book "Investing for Dummies" helped me understand my finances better. Premise: The book "Chess for Dummies" was written by the same author, published by the same press, and costs about the same amount. Conclusion: "Chess for Dummies" would probably help me understand my finances as well.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because two books share some superficial attributes (author, publisher, price), they will also share a functional benefit (improving financial understanding), ignoring that the content of the books is entirely different (investing vs. chess).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is False Analogy. Canonical form: [A] and [B] share property [P]. [A] has property [Q]. Therefore, [B] has property [Q]. Instantiation: ["Investing for Dummies"] and ["Chess for Dummies"] share property [same author, publisher, price]. ["Investing for Dummies"] has property [helps understand finances]. Therefore, ["Chess for Dummies"] has property [helps understand finances].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the shared attributes (author, publisher, price) are irrelevant to the functional outcome; the subject matter of the books is different, so the benefit from one does not logically transfer to the other.

source_article:
Person 1:
I am for raising the minimum wage in our state.
Person 2:
