source_article: I go to my front porch every morning and yell, “May no tigers enter this house!” and for 20 years, not a single tiger has entered my house. My tiger prevention strategy clearly works.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I yell “May no tigers enter this house!” every morning and for 20 years, no tiger has entered my house. Conclusion: My tiger prevention strategy works.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because one event (yelling a phrase) precedes another event (no tigers entering), the first event must be the cause of the second, ignoring the fact that tigers are not native to the area and would never enter anyway.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Yelling a tiger-prevention phrase] occurred, then [no tigers entered the house]. Therefore, [yelling the phrase] caused [no tigers entering].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the absence of tigers is almost certainly due to geographic and ecological factors (tigers do not live near the house), not the yelling, and mere temporal correlation does not prove causation.

source_article: In the 6 years that I have been practicing my new and improved brand of cognitive-humanistic-dynamic-behavioral-deconstructive-metaregressive-deontological psychotherapy (now with biofeedback!), which I developed, there has not been one published study showing that it fails to work or that it has ever harmed a patient. It is clearly one of the safest and most effective interventions ever devised.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: In the 6 years that I have been practicing my new and improved brand of psychotherapy, there has not been one published study showing that it fails to work or that it has ever harmed a patient. Conclusion: It is clearly one of the safest and most effective interventions ever devised.

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker uses the absence of negative studies as proof of safety and effectiveness, showing an intent to win the argument by an appeal to ignorance rather than providing positive evidence from controlled studies.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The therapist knows that a lack of negative studies does not prove safety or effectiveness. The therapist still claims the therapy is one of the safest and most effective interventions using an argument from ignorance.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (absence of published negative studies) does not logically imply the therapy is safe or effective; it could simply mean no studies have been conducted, or positive studies are suppressed, showing an intentional shift of the burden of proof.

source_article: If you don’t say the Pledge of Allegiance, then you must be a traitor.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: [If you don’t say the Pledge of Allegiance, then you must be a traitor.]. Conclusion: [You are either a patriot who says the pledge or a traitor who doesn’t.].

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument falsely restricts the options to only two possibilities—either saying the pledge (patriot) or not saying it (traitor)—while ignoring many other legitimate reasons for not saying it, such as personal belief, protest, or physical inability.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you say the Pledge of Allegiance (patriot) or you are a traitor.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because there are numerous alternatives to not saying the pledge that do not equate to treason, so the forced binary choice is logically invalid.

source_article:
Person 1: You’re either for the war or against the troops.

Person 2: Actually, I do not want our troops sent into a dangerous war.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "You’re either for the war or against the troops." Conclusion: There is no other position.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker presents only two mutually exclusive options—supporting the war or opposing the troops—ignoring other possible stances, such as opposing the war while still supporting the troops.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either [for the war] or [against the troops] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that these are the only two options does not follow, because one can oppose the war (for example, by not wanting troops sent into danger) while still supporting the troops, thus the premise artificially restricts the available positions.

source_article: "Either you pass this test, or you fail it." Laughed Sr Malak
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: There are two options about the test outcome. Conclusion: Only either passing or failing is possible.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker presents only two mutually exclusive outcomes for a test (pass or fail) when tests often allow partial scores, retakes, or other results.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you pass this test or you fail it.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that only two outcomes exist does not follow from the premise because tests can have other results (e.g., incomplete, conditional pass, retake) so the dichotomy is false.

source_article: If you don't go to camp, you won't have fun this summer, and your whole summer will be ruined!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If you don't go to camp, you won't have fun this summer. Conclusion: Your whole summer will be ruined.

Step 2 - Pattern: The arguer assumes that not going to camp leads to no fun at all, and then generalizes that a lack of fun ruins the entire summer, ignoring other possible sources of fun.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] (not going to camp) leads to [MSK2] (no fun). [MSK2] is generalized to [MSK3] (ruined summer). Instantiation: Not going to camp leads to no fun; no fun is generalized to a ruined summer.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only addresses one activity (camp) and assumes it is the sole source of fun, ignoring other activities that could provide enjoyment and prevent a ruined summer.

source_article: Is your stupidity inborn?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The question "Is your stupidity inborn?" is asked. Conclusion: The speaker intends to win the argument by using an insult rather than providing supporting evidence.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker uses a loaded question that assumes the listener is stupid and that this stupidity is a trait (inborn), which is an attempt to discredit the listener without any factual basis, showing an intent to win the argument through rhetorical attack rather than logical reasoning.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows the question is a fallacy that assumes the listener's stupidity without evidence, yet still uses it to claim the listener is wrong, using an incorrect argument (loaded question).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (asking the question) does not provide any evidence for the listener's stupidity or for any argument; it is an intentional rhetorical tactic to shift the burden of proof onto the listener.

source_article: The writer, speaker, or an ad supports a claim with restatements of the same claim. (The argument goes around and around in a circle)
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The writer, speaker, or an ad supports a claim with restatements of the same claim. Conclusion: The argument goes around and around in a circle.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text defines circular reasoning by describing its own process—supporting a claim with restatements of the same claim—which is itself a circular restatement of the definition.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: The claim about circular reasoning is supported by restating that it involves restating the same claim, which is circular.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise merely describes the fallacy without providing any independent evidence or reasoning; it assumes what it is trying to prove.

source_article: Of course you don't like rap or hip-hop music! You wear the same clothes every day and have zero fashion sense.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: You wear the same clothes every day and have zero fashion sense. Conclusion: Therefore, you don't like rap or hip-hop music.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks the person's clothing habits and fashion sense instead of addressing any reasons for or against liking rap or hip-hop music.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a moron. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The person is claiming you don't like rap or hip-hop music. The person wears the same clothes every day and has zero fashion sense. Therefore, your dislike is invalid or wrong.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion about the person's musical taste does not follow from the premise about their fashion sense, because clothing habits are irrelevant to musical preferences.

source_article: It is said that we have a good understanding of our universe.  Therefore, we know exactly how it began and exactly when.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: It is said that we have a good understanding of our universe. Conclusion: Therefore, we know exactly how it began and exactly when.
Step 2 - Pattern: The phrase "good understanding" in the premise suggests a broad, general, or incomplete knowledge, but in the conclusion it is reinterpreted as "exact knowledge" of specific details (how and when the universe began), shifting from a vague or partial understanding to a precise and complete one.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: "good understanding" is used to mean a general, possibly incomplete grasp in the premise. "good understanding" is used to mean exact, detailed knowledge in the conclusion.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because a general or good understanding does not entail exact knowledge of specific origins; the meaning of "good understanding" shifts from a broad sense to a precise one.

source_article: Before he died, poet Allen Ginsberg argued in favor of legalizing pornography. But Ginsberg's arguments are nothing but trash. Ginsberg was a pot-smoking homosexual and a thoroughgoing advocate of the drug culture.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Allen Ginsberg argued in favor of legalizing pornography. Premise: Ginsberg was a pot-smoking homosexual and an advocate of the drug culture. Conclusion: Ginsberg's arguments are nothing but trash (i.e., not valid or false).

Step 2 - Pattern: Instead of addressing the merits of Ginsberg's arguments for legalizing pornography, the speaker attacks Ginsberg's personal characteristics (his drug use and sexual orientation) as a reason to dismiss his arguments.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a [negative personal attribute]. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: [Allen Ginsberg] is claiming [pornography should be legalized]. [Allen Ginsberg] is a pot-smoking homosexual and drug culture advocate. Therefore, [Ginsberg's arguments for legalizing pornography] are not true (trash).

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Ginsberg's arguments are worthless does not follow from the premise about his personal life; his sexual orientation and drug use are irrelevant to the logical validity or factual accuracy of his arguments for legalizing pornography.

source_article: (Ignoring the Question): when a rebuttal doesn't address the question
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The text defines "Ignoring the Question" as "when a rebuttal doesn't address the question." Conclusion: This definition describes a fallacy of relevance.

Step 2 - Pattern: The text presents a definition of a fallacy where the rebuttal fails to address the original question, which is a classic red herring tactic that diverts attention from the main issue by bringing in unrelated material or ignoring the query entirely.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that "a rebuttal that doesn't address the question" implies a valid counterargument, whereas failing to address the question is unrelated to providing relevant evidence or refutation.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that this definition exemplifies the fallacy of relevance does not follow because the text merely defines the fallacy without presenting an actual argument or rebuttal; it is a description, not an instance of fallacious reasoning.

source_article:
A: I don't want to visit McDonald's today.
B: So you're just against good food then?
The above is an example of:
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: A says "I don't want to visit McDonald's today." Conclusion: B claims A is "just against good food then."

Step 2 - Pattern: B distorts A's specific, temporary preference (not wanting to go to McDonald's today) into a general, absolute stance (being against all good food), then attacks that distorted position instead of addressing the original statement.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: A makes claim "I don't want to visit McDonald's today." B restates it as "you're just against good food" (distorted to a general opposition). B attacks the distorted version (implying it's unreasonable). Therefore, A's original preference is implied to be false or unreasonable.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that A is against good food does not follow from the premise, because not wanting to visit McDonald's on one occasion does not mean rejecting all good food; B has replaced a specific, mild claim with an exaggerated, absolute version.

source_article: "I'd like to punch him in the face." - Donald Trump (Feb. 2016, at a campaign rally, remarks directed at a protester who was being escorted out of the rally.)
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: A protester was being escorted out of a rally. Conclusion: "I'd like to punch him in the face."

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker expresses a violent desire, which is an emotional reaction (anger/aggression), rather than offering a reasoned argument or evidence against the protester's actions.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: The desire to punch the protester is made without evidence. In place of evidence, an emotional appeal to anger or aggression is used to convince the audience that violence is a justified response.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that punching the protester is appropriate does not follow from the premise that the protester was being escorted out; the statement relies on an emotional reaction rather than logical reasoning or factual justification.

source_article: Senator Ribbit is a liar. You shouldn't listen to him on anything.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Senator Ribbit is a liar. Conclusion: You shouldn't listen to him on anything.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the person (Senator Ribbit) by calling him a liar, rather than addressing any specific claim or position he might hold.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a liar. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Senator Ribbit is claiming something. Senator Ribbit is a liar. Therefore, what he claims is not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because being a liar does not logically entail that every statement he makes is false; the argument ignores the content of his claims.

source_article: There's no way you know this - you're clearly too young to understand!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The person is too young to understand. Conclusion: Therefore, what they know is not valid.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks the person's age instead of addressing the content of their claim or knowledge.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is too young. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The speaker is claiming knowledge. The speaker is too young. Therefore, the knowledge is not valid.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the person's knowledge is invalid does not follow from the premise of their age, as age does not determine the truth of a claim.

source_article: Mrs. Sedik really knows math; she would be an excellent math instructor.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Mrs. Sedik really knows math. Conclusion: She would be an excellent math instructor.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that knowing math is sufficient to be an excellent math instructor, ignoring other necessary qualities like teaching ability, communication skills, and patience.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Non sequitur. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true. Therefore, [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: [Mrs. Sedik really knows math] is true. Therefore, [she would be an excellent math instructor] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because being knowledgeable in a subject does not guarantee teaching excellence; many other factors are required for effective instruction.

source_article: "I asked my five closest friends and they all agree that the new Underwater Basketweaving Team Coach is a jerk, therefore I'm not trying out for the team" IS an example of THIS fallacy.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I asked my five closest friends and they all agree that the new Underwater Basketweaving Team Coach is a jerk. Conclusion: Therefore I'm not trying out for the team.  
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker draws a conclusion about the coach's character based on a small, non-representative sample (five friends) and then uses that to justify a personal decision not to try out.  
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] (five friends) has attribute [MSK2] (agree coach is a jerk). [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3] (all people who know the coach). Therefore, all [MSK3] (all people or the coach's character) has attribute [MSK2] (coach is a jerk). Instantiation: Five friends agree coach is a jerk; these friends are a subset of all people who know the coach; therefore, the coach must be a jerk.  
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the coach is a jerk (and thus the speaker should not try out) does not follow from the premise because the sample of five friends is too small and may be biased, so it cannot reliably represent the coach's true character.

source_article: Dr. X is an engineer, and he doesn’t believe in global warming.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Dr. X is an engineer. Conclusion: He doesn’t believe in global warming.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument relies on Dr. X's status as an engineer to lend credibility to his disbelief in global warming, but engineering expertise does not make him an authority in climate science, making this an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] are experts in the field concerning [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] should be believed. Instantiation: [MSK1] = Dr. X (an engineer), [MSK2] = global warming is not real.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because being an engineer does not qualify Dr. X as an expert in climate science; his personal disbelief does not constitute evidence against global warming.

source_article: Decriminalizing marijuana turns the U.S. into a stoner nation
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Decriminalizing marijuana is a policy change. Conclusion: Decriminalizing marijuana turns the U.S. into a stoner nation.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes a single policy change (decriminalization) and assumes it will cause a broad, extreme outcome (the entire nation becoming "stoners"), without evidence that this effect applies to all or most people.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] (decriminalization) has attribute [MSK2] (causes stoner behavior). [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3] (policy changes). Therefore, all [MSK3] (policy changes) have attribute [MSK2] (turn the U.S. into a stoner nation). Instantiation: Decriminalizing marijuana leads to widespread stoner behavior; decriminalization is a policy change; therefore, all policy changes (or specifically this one) turn the U.S. into a stoner nation.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because decriminalizing marijuana does not logically imply that the entire nation will become "stoners"; it overgeneralizes from a specific policy to an extreme, unsupported outcome, ignoring other factors like personal choice, regulation, and cultural norms.

source_article: "When my parents talk to me about how important it is to learn a second language, I remind them they didn't learn another language.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My parents didn't learn a second language. Conclusion: Therefore, their advice about learning a second language is invalid.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker dismisses their parents' advice by pointing out that the parents themselves do not follow it, implicitly assuming that only those who practice a behavior can validly advocate for it, which is a form of circular reasoning because the rejection of the advice is based on a premise that itself undermines the argument's validity without independent justification.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is not valid because [MSK2] does not do [MSK1]. [MSK2] does not do [MSK1] because [MSK1] is not valid. Instantiation: The parents' advice is not valid because they didn't learn a second language. They didn't learn a second language because their advice is not valid.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the parents' advice is invalid does not follow from the premise that they didn't learn a second language, as the premise does not address the truth or usefulness of the advice itself, only the speakers' actions.

source_article: People generally like to walk on the beach. Beaches have sand. Therefore, having sand floors in homes would be a great idea!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: People generally like to walk on the beach. Premise: Beaches have sand. Conclusion: Having sand floors in homes would be a great idea.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument draws a conclusion about a different context (sand floors in homes) based on a property of beaches (sand and people liking to walk there), but it ignores that other features of beaches (e.g., ocean, breeze, open space) likely contribute to the enjoyment, and that sand in a home would have different practical consequences (e.g., mess, discomfort indoors).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is False Analogy. Canonical form: [MSK1] and [MSK2] are similar in some respects, so they are similar in the relevant respect. Instantiation: Beaches and homes are similar in having sand, so they are similar in being enjoyable places for walking.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the analogy between a beach and a home with sand floors is weak; the features that make beach walking enjoyable are not solely due to sand, and sand indoors creates different, likely negative, experiences.

source_article: Look, it doesn't matter whether or not you think America should build a wall on the border. The President thinks America should do it, so it's a good idea.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The President thinks America should build a wall on the border. Conclusion: Building a wall on the border is a good idea.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the President's opinion as sufficient reason to accept the conclusion, without providing independent evidence or reasoning, which is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is good. Instantiation: [MSK1] = The President, [MSK2] = building a wall on the border.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the President's opinion alone does not establish the goodness or correctness of the policy; the argument lacks substantive reasons, evidence, or ethical justification for why the wall is a good idea.

source_article:
Speaker 1: I really think we need to do something about the rising levels of poverty and homelessness in our country.
Speaker 2: Why are you worried about poverty? Look how many children we abort every day.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Speaker 2 says "Look how many children we abort every day." Conclusion: This statement is intended to counter Speaker 1's argument about rising poverty and homelessness.

Step 2 - Pattern: Speaker 2 introduces an unrelated topic about abortion to divert attention from Speaker 1's argument about poverty and homelessness, rather than addressing why action on poverty is unnecessary or misguided.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that the number of abortions implies that poverty and homelessness are not a concern, whereas abortion rates are unrelated to the issue of poverty and homelessness.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Speaker 2's response counters Speaker 1's point does not follow because the frequency of abortions is irrelevant to the need to address poverty and homelessness; it merely shifts focus away from the original issue.

source_article: Mother: It’s bedtime Jane Jane: Mom, how do ants feed their babies? Mother: Don’t know dear, close your eyes now. Jane: But mama, do ant babies cry when they’re hungry?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Jane asks how ants feed their babies and whether ant babies cry when hungry. Conclusion: This question is intended to delay bedtime by diverting her mother's attention.

Step 2 - Pattern: Jane introduces an unrelated topic about ant biology to divert attention from her mother's instruction that it is bedtime, rather than addressing why she should stay awake or go to bed.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that asking about ant feeding implies that bedtime should be delayed, whereas ant feeding is unrelated to the bedtime schedule.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Jane's questions justify delaying bedtime does not follow because the topic of ant feeding is irrelevant to the mother's directive about bedtime; it merely shifts focus away from the immediate issue of going to sleep.

source_article:
All Catholics are Christian.
All Christians are Jesus lovers.
Therefore, all Jesus lovers are Catholic.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: All Catholics are Christian. Premise: All Christians are Jesus lovers. Conclusion: All Jesus lovers are Catholic.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument incorrectly reverses the direction of the categorical relationship. It assumes that if all A are B and all B are C, then all C are A, but this is a logical error because the relationship is not symmetric.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Undistributed Middle. Canonical form: All [MSK1] are [MSK2]. All [MSK2] are [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] are [MSK1]. Instantiation: All Catholics are Christian. All Christians are Jesus lovers. Therefore, all Jesus lovers are Catholic.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premises only establish that Catholics are a subset of Christians and Christians are a subset of Jesus lovers, but Jesus lovers may include people who are not Catholic (e.g., other Christian denominations).

source_article: "You can't believe that theory. No one would believe that. It's too unbelievable."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The speaker says "No one would believe that" and "It's too unbelievable." Conclusion: This is meant to refute the theory itself.

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks the theory by citing its perceived incredibility or lack of popular acceptance, rather than providing evidence against its truth or logical coherence.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that the theory's being unbelievable implies it is false, whereas the theory's believability is unrelated to its truth.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the theory is false does not follow from the premise that it is unbelievable, because a claim's truth is independent of how many people believe it or how implausible it sounds.

source_article: "The critical mission was horribly executed." is an example of ...
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The critical mission was horribly executed. Conclusion: The statement is an example of appeal to emotion.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text asserts a claim about the mission being "horribly executed" but does not provide evidence; instead, it relies on the emotionally charged word "horribly" to evoke a negative emotional response, making the audience accept the claim without logical support.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: "The critical mission was horribly executed" is made without evidence. In place of evidence, the emotionally loaded word "horribly" is used to convince the interlocutor that the mission was poorly performed.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the text merely states an emotional judgment ("horribly executed") without any factual evidence or reasoning to support why the mission was executed poorly.

source_article:
All Paul Newman movies are great.
All great movies are Oscar winners.
Therefore, all Oscar winners are Paul Newman movies.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: All Paul Newman movies are great. Premise: All great movies are Oscar winners. Conclusion: All Oscar winners are Paul Newman movies.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument reverses the direction of the categorical syllogism, assuming that if all A are B and all B are C, then all C are A, which is invalid because the relationship is one-way.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Affirming the Consequent (in the context of categorical logic). Canonical form: All [MSK1] are [MSK2]. All [MSK2] are [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] are [MSK1]. Instantiation: All Paul Newman movies are great. All great movies are Oscar winners. Therefore, all Oscar winners are Paul Newman movies.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premises only establish that Paul Newman movies are a subset of great movies, and great movies are a subset of Oscar winners; they do not imply that all Oscar winners are Paul Newman movies, as there could be many other great movies that are not Paul Newman films.

source_article:
“The police should stop environmental demonstrators from inconveniencing the general public. We pay our taxes.”
“Surely global meltdown is infinitely worse than a little inconvenience?”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The second speaker says "Surely global meltdown is infinitely worse than a little inconvenience?" Conclusion: This statement is intended to counter the first speaker's argument that police should stop environmental demonstrators from inconveniencing the general public because we pay taxes.

Step 2 - Pattern: The second speaker introduces a new, extreme point about global meltdown to divert attention from the first speaker's original argument about the justification for police intervention based on tax payment, rather than addressing whether paying taxes entitles the public to avoid inconvenience.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that the relative severity of global meltdown implies that the police should not stop demonstrators for inconveniencing the public, whereas the severity of global meltdown is unrelated to the specific claim about tax payment and inconvenience.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the second speaker's response counters the first speaker's point does not follow because the comparison to global meltdown is irrelevant to whether paying taxes justifies police action against minor inconveniences; it merely shifts focus away from the original argument.

source_article: “President Kumail raised taxes, and then the rate of violent crime went up. Kumail is responsible for the rise in crime.”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: President Kumail raised taxes, and then the rate of violent crime went up. Conclusion: Kumail is responsible for the rise in crime.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because one event (raising taxes) preceded another event (increase in crime), the first event must have caused the second, without considering other possible factors that could explain the crime rise.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [President Kumail raised taxes] occurred, then [rate of violent crime went up]. Therefore, [Kumail] caused [the rise in crime].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because mere temporal succession does not prove causation; the rise in crime could be due to other factors such as changes in policing, economic conditions, or demographic shifts, and no evidence of a causal link is provided.

source_article: My friend said her Math class was hard, and the one I’m in is hard, too. All Math classes must be hard!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My friend said her Math class was hard, and the one I’m in is hard, too. Conclusion: All Math classes must be hard.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes two specific instances of Math classes being hard and concludes that every Math class shares that same attribute, ignoring the possibility of variation among classes.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically, hasty generalization). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [MSK1] = "the two Math classes mentioned", [MSK2] = "hard", [MSK3] = "all Math classes".
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because two instances are insufficient to prove that every Math class is hard; many other Math classes may differ in difficulty.

source_article: Studies show that it takes up to seven years for the human body to digest a piece of gum.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Studies show that it takes up to seven years for the human body to digest a piece of gum. Conclusion: It takes up to seven years for the human body to digest a piece of gum.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the authority of "studies" without specifying which studies, who conducted them, or their credibility, which is a vague appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: [MSK1] = unspecified "studies", [MSK2] = it takes up to seven years for the human body to digest a piece of gum.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise lacks specific, credible sources; the claim is widely debunked by medical experts, so citing vague "studies" does not provide reliable evidence.

source_article:
Parent: I really don’t want you to smoke pot. It’s still illegal, and could get you into trouble.
Child: Didn’t you smoke pot when you were my age? You must not think it’s a big deal.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The parent smoked pot when they were the child's age. Conclusion: The parent must not think smoking pot is a big deal.
Step 2 - Pattern: The child attacks the parent's argument by pointing out the parent's past hypocrisy, instead of addressing the parent's actual reasons (legality and potential trouble).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem (tu quoque subtype). Canonical form: [MSK1] claims [MSK2] is wrong. [MSK1] did [MSK2] themselves. Therefore, [MSK2] is not wrong. Instantiation: The parent claims smoking pot is wrong. The parent smoked pot themselves. Therefore, smoking pot is not wrong.
Step 4 - Verdict: The parent's past actions do not refute their current argument about legality and potential trouble; the conclusion about the parent's belief does not logically follow from the premise of their past behavior.

source_article: Two words: New Coke.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: [Implied: There is a product called "New Coke".] Conclusion: [No explicit conclusion is given; the text is a bare appeal to the name "New Coke" as a cautionary example.]
Step 2 - Pattern: The text uses the name "New Coke" as a shorthand appeal to a historical example of a failed product launch, implying that the mere mention of the name is sufficient to discredit or warn against something without providing reasoning. This is an appeal to authority (specifically, the authority of a well-known historical failure) and thus a fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] is a known failed example. Therefore, [MSK2] should be avoided or is wrong. Instantiation: [MSK1] = New Coke, [MSK2] = any similar product or idea being compared.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because merely invoking the name of a historical failure does not provide a logical reason to reject a current proposal; it assumes a false equivalence without evidence or argument.

source_article: You must obey the law, because it's illegal to break the law.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: It's illegal to break the law. Conclusion: You must obey the law.
Step 2 - Pattern: The premise and conclusion are essentially the same claim expressed in different words, so the argument provides no independent support for the conclusion.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: You must obey the law is true because it's illegal to break the law. It's illegal to break the law is true because you must obey the law.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion "you must obey the law" does not follow from the premise "it's illegal to break the law" because the premise is simply a restatement of the conclusion, offering no new evidence or reasoning to justify it.

source_article: "Four legs good, two legs bad"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The majority of animals in the story accept the slogan "Four legs good, two legs bad." Conclusion: Therefore, the slogan is true or correct.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the widespread acceptance of the slogan by the animal community as evidence of its truth, rather than providing independent reasoning or evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of animals believe "Four legs good, two legs bad." Therefore, "Four legs good, two legs bad" must be true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the popularity of a belief does not guarantee its truth; the slogan could be false even if widely accepted, as it is a political propaganda tool in the story.

source_article:
Gus is Christian or Gus is politically liberal.
Gus is a Christian.
Therefore, Gus is not politically liberal.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Gus is Christian or Gus is politically liberal. Premise: Gus is a Christian. Conclusion: Gus is not politically liberal.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that if one disjunct (Gus is Christian) is true, the other disjunct (Gus is politically liberal) must be false, ignoring that the "or" could be inclusive, allowing both to be true.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Affirming the Disjunct. Canonical form: [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. [MSK1] is true. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Gus is Christian or Gus is politically liberal. Gus is Christian. Therefore, Gus is not politically liberal.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise uses an inclusive "or" (logical disjunction), which does not exclude the possibility that Gus is both Christian and politically liberal; thus, affirming one disjunct does not logically negate the other.

source_article: If we get rid of assault weapons for citizens then we will also soon lose freedom of speech and all freedoms as other countries rush in to conquer us (if the zombies don’t exterminate us first!)
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If we get rid of assault weapons for citizens, then we will also soon lose freedom of speech and all freedoms as other countries rush in to conquer us (if the zombies don’t exterminate us first!). Conclusion: Therefore, getting rid of assault weapons leads to a total loss of freedoms and national conquest.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that a single policy change (removing assault weapons) will inevitably cause a chain of extreme, unrelated consequences (loss of all freedoms, invasion, zombie apocalypse) without evidence or logical connection, generalizing from one step to a catastrophic outcome.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically a slippery slope). Canonical form: If [MSK1] occurs, then [MSK2] will happen, and then [MSK3], leading to [MSK4]. Instantiation: If we get rid of assault weapons, then we lose freedom of speech, then all freedoms, then other countries conquer us (and zombies exterminate us).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only addresses a specific restriction on assault weapons, but the conclusion leaps to unrelated, extreme outcomes (loss of all freedoms, invasion, zombie extinction) without any causal evidence or logical necessity, making it a hasty generalization of consequences.

source_article: Supreme Court Justice Byron White was an All-American football player while in college, so how can you say that athletes are dumb?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Supreme Court Justice Byron White was an All-American football player while in college. Conclusion: Athletes are not dumb.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses a single counterexample (Byron White) to dismiss a generalization about all athletes being dumb, ignoring that one exceptional case does not disprove a general tendency.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Byron White is not dumb. Byron White is an athlete. Therefore, all athletes are not dumb.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that athletes are not dumb does not follow from the premise because one intelligent athlete does not prove that all athletes are intelligent; the sample is too small and unrepresentative.

source_article:
Jen: Don’t you realize that all this drinking you are doing is making your family miserable?
Bridget: Yes, I do.
Jen: Then what are you doing about it?
Bridget: Drinking to forget.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Jen observes that Bridget’s drinking is making her family miserable and asks what Bridget is doing about it. Conclusion: Bridget says she is drinking to forget (i.e., she drinks to cope with the misery caused by drinking).
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument implies that drinking is the solution to the problem it creates, but this reasoning assumes a causal link where drinking (the supposed cause of misery) also serves as the cause of forgetting, without evidence that drinking actually resolves the misery.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Drinking] occurred, then [misery] occurred. Therefore, [drinking] caused [forgetting the misery].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only establishes that drinking is associated with misery, not that drinking causes forgetting of that misery; the causal relationship is circular and unsupported, as drinking may worsen the problem rather than solve it.

source_article: Aliens must exist because most people believe in them.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Most people believe in aliens. Conclusion: Aliens must exist.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the popularity of a belief as evidence for its truth, rather than providing independent evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people believe aliens exist. Therefore, aliens must exist.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the mere fact that many people hold a belief does not make it true; truth requires empirical evidence or logical proof, not consensus.

source_article: Of course the Senator from Maine opposes a reduction in naval spending. After all, Bath Ironworks, which produces warships, is in Maine.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The Senator from Maine opposes a reduction in naval spending. Bath Ironworks, which produces warships, is in Maine. Conclusion: Therefore, the Senator's opposition is invalid or should be dismissed.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the Senator's position by attributing it to a biased motive (local economic interest) rather than engaging with the merits of the naval spending reduction.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem (circumstantial). Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2] because [MSK1] has a personal interest in [MSK3]. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The Senator is claiming that a reduction in naval spending is bad. The Senator has a personal interest in Bath Ironworks being in Maine. Therefore, the Senator's opposition is not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the Senator's local economic interest does not disprove the validity of the argument against reducing naval spending; the argument attacks the person's motive instead of addressing the policy's merits.

source_article: "If you don't get an A in Mr. K's class you will fail high school, never graduate, and end up fighting people for nickels under freeway as a career"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Not getting an A in Mr. K's class leads to failing high school, not graduating, and a poor career. Conclusion: Therefore, not getting an A in Mr. K's class will inevitably result in that extreme outcome for all students.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker takes a single possible consequence (or a few cases) of not getting an A and generalizes it to a universal, inevitable, and extreme outcome for everyone in that situation.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically Hasty Generalization). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Not getting an A in Mr. K's class (MSK1) leads to failing high school and a poor career (MSK2). Not getting an A in Mr. K's class (MSK1) is a subset of all academic outcomes (MSK3). Therefore, all academic outcomes (MSK3) that involve not getting an A in Mr. K's class lead to failing high school and a poor career.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only describes one possible negative outcome (or a few cases) of not getting an A, but it does not provide evidence that this extreme chain of events occurs for all students or is inevitable. Many students who do not get an A in a single class still graduate and have successful careers.

source_article: I heard that the Catholic Church was involved in a sex scandal cover-up.  Therefore, my 102-year-old Catholic neighbor, who frequently attends Church, is guilty as well!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The Catholic Church was involved in a sex scandal cover-up. Conclusion: Therefore, my 102-year-old Catholic neighbor, who frequently attends Church, is guilty as well.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes a specific incident involving an organization (the Catholic Church) and incorrectly attributes guilt to an individual member of that organization without any evidence linking them to the incident.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically, Guilt by Association). Canonical form: [MSK1] is a member of group [MSK2]. Group [MSK2] has attribute [MSK3]. Therefore, [MSK1] has attribute [MSK3]. Instantiation: The Catholic Church has the attribute of being involved in a sex scandal cover-up. My neighbor is a member of the Catholic Church. Therefore, my neighbor has the attribute of being guilty as well.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because membership in a group does not automatically transfer the actions or attributes of some members (or the group as a whole) to every individual member, especially without any direct evidence linking the neighbor to the scandal.

source_article:
Mike: It is morally wrong to cheat on your spouse, why on earth would you have done that?

Ken: But what is morality exactly?

Mike: It’s a code of conduct shared by cultures.

Ken: But who creates this code?...
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Ken asks "But what is morality exactly?" and then "But who creates this code?" Conclusion: These questions are intended to divert from Mike's argument that cheating on one's spouse is morally wrong.

Step 2 - Pattern: Instead of addressing the moral claim about cheating, Ken shifts the discussion to abstract meta-ethical questions about the nature and origin of morality, which are irrelevant to the specific issue of whether cheating is wrong.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that questioning the definition or source of morality implies that the moral wrongness of cheating is not established, whereas the definition or source of morality is unrelated to the specific moral judgment about cheating.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Ken's questions undermine Mike's argument does not follow because exploring the abstract foundations of morality is irrelevant to the concrete claim that cheating is morally wrong; it merely changes the subject.

source_article: I think that we should reject what Father Jones has to say about the ethical issues of abortion because he is a Catholic priest. After all, Father Jones is required to hold such views
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Father Jones is a Catholic priest and is required to hold such views. Conclusion: We should reject what Father Jones has to say about the ethical issues of abortion.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the person making the claim (Father Jones) based on his role as a Catholic priest and his supposed obligation to hold those views, rather than addressing the substance of his arguments about abortion ethics.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem (genetic fallacy subtype). Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a Catholic priest required to hold such views. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Father Jones is claiming ethical issues about abortion. Father Jones is a Catholic priest required to hold such views. Therefore, his claims about abortion ethics should be rejected.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because Father Jones's status as a Catholic priest or his required views do not logically invalidate the truth or validity of his arguments about abortion ethics; the argument should be assessed on its own merits.

source_article:
Speaker 1: You should trust the Bible because it’s the Word of God.
Speaker 2: How do you know it’s the Word of God?
Speaker 1: Because God tells us it is.
Speaker 2: Where does God tell us this?
Speaker 1: Right here, in the Bible.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The Bible is the Word of God because God tells us it is. Conclusion: The Bible is the Word of God.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses the Bible as its own evidence, where the claim that the Bible is God's word is supported by a statement from the Bible itself, which is only authoritative if it is already God's word.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: The Bible is the Word of God because God tells us it is. God tells us it is because it says so in the Bible.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise assumes the very authority it is trying to prove, creating a loop with no external justification.

source_article: If you don't say the Pledge of Allegiance you are a traitor.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: [You don't say the Pledge of Allegiance.] Conclusion: [You are a traitor.]
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker presents only two possible positions—saying the Pledge or being a traitor—ignoring other reasons for not saying it, such as personal belief, protest, or inconvenience.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you say the Pledge of Allegiance or you are a traitor.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because there are many legitimate reasons not to say the Pledge (e.g., pacifism, religious objections, silent protest) that do not make one a traitor.

source_article: I have flipped heads five times in a row.  As a result, the next flip will probably be tails.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I have flipped heads five times in a row. Conclusion: As a result, the next flip will probably be tails.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because a streak of heads has occurred, the probability of tails increases to balance the sequence, which is a misunderstanding of independent events in probability.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] is expected to occur. Therefore, [MSK1] caused the expectation of [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Flipping heads five times in a row] occurred, then [the next flip will probably be tails]. Therefore, [the streak of heads] caused the expectation of tails.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because each coin flip is independent; the previous flips have no causal effect on the next flip's outcome, and the probability of tails remains 50% regardless of past results.

source_article: blame opponent's policy for the country's problems
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The opponent's policy is blamed for the country's problems. Conclusion: The opponent's policy caused the country's problems.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes a causal link between the opponent's policy and the country's problems simply by associating them, without providing evidence that the policy directly caused the problems.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Opponent's policy] occurred, then [country's problems] occurred. Therefore, [opponent's policy] caused [country's problems].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise merely asserts blame without evidence of causation; the country's problems could be due to other factors such as external events, previous policies, or unrelated issues.

source_article: Don't be out-dated and un-fashionable! Buy the new, modern double-lined heavy duty jeans - the old ripped jeans of the past are out!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "Don't be out-dated and un-fashionable! Buy the new, modern double-lined heavy duty jeans - the old ripped jeans of the past are out!" Conclusion: You should buy the new jeans because the old ones are out of style.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker uses emotional appeal (fear of being outdated) and a false dichotomy (old vs. new) without providing any evidence that the new jeans are actually better in quality or function, showing an intent to win the argument by manipulating the listener rather than supporting the claim.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The advertiser knows that "out-dated" and "un-fashionable" are subjective labels, not evidence of inferiority. The advertiser still claims the old jeans are out using an incorrect argument (appeal to fashion trends).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (fear of being outdated) does not logically prove the new jeans are a better purchase; the argument intentionally avoids providing objective evidence and relies on emotional manipulation.

source_article: A commercial claims that a specific brand of cereal is the best way to start the day because athlete Michael Jordan says that it is what he eats every day for breakfast.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Michael Jordan says that he eats this brand of cereal every day for breakfast. Conclusion: This brand of cereal is the best way to start the day.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the endorsement of a celebrity athlete (Michael Jordan) as a reason to believe the product is superior, which is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility, but the authority is not an expert in nutrition or breakfast cereal.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2] is good. [MSK1] is a famous person. Therefore, [MSK2] is the best choice. Instantiation: [MSK1] = Michael Jordan, [MSK2] = this brand of cereal.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because Michael Jordan’s fame as an athlete does not make him an expert on nutrition or breakfast cereals; his personal choice does not prove the cereal is objectively the best way to start the day for everyone.

source_article: A misrepresentation of an opponent’s position that makes it easier to argue against
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The text describes a misrepresentation of an opponent’s position that makes it easier to argue against. Conclusion: This misrepresentation is the fallacy itself.
Step 2 - Pattern: The definition directly states the core of the straw man fallacy: distorting an opponent’s position to a weaker version for easier attack, without providing a specific argument instance.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: The text is a definition, not an argument; it describes the pattern where an opponent’s position is misrepresented to make it easier to argue against.
Step 4 - Verdict: The definition does not present a conclusion from a premise; it merely describes the fallacy pattern, so there is no logical failure to evaluate.

source_article: We as the Board can either choose to approve this initial public offering or we can suffer slow annihilation at the hands of our competitors.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The Board can either approve the initial public offering or suffer slow annihilation at the hands of competitors. Conclusion: These are the only two choices available.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument presents only two mutually exclusive options (approve IPO or suffer annihilation) and ignores other possible alternatives, such as seeking partnerships, cutting costs, or issuing debt.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either [approve the initial public offering] or [suffer slow annihilation at the hands of competitors] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that only these two options exist does not follow from the premise because many other strategic choices could avoid both annihilation and the IPO, so the dichotomy is false.

source_article: Plagiarism is deceitful because it is dishonest.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Plagiarism is dishonest. Conclusion: Plagiarism is deceitful.
Step 2 - Pattern: The conclusion restates the premise using synonymous terms ("deceitful" and "dishonest"), with no independent evidence to support either claim.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: Plagiarism is deceitful because it is dishonest. Plagiarism is dishonest because it is deceitful.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise and conclusion are equivalent in meaning; no new information or reasoning is provided to justify the claim.

source_article:
Bert: How do eyes project an image to your brain?
Ernie: Think of it as a little guy in your brain watching the movie projected by your eyes.
Bert: Ok, but what is happening in the little guy in your head’s brain?
Ernie: Well, think of it as a little guy in his brain watching a movie...
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The explanation of vision involves a little guy in your brain watching a movie projected by your eyes. Conclusion: The same explanation applies to the little guy's brain, requiring another little guy, leading to an infinite regress.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument explains a phenomenon by positing an internal homunculus, but when asked to explain how that homunculus perceives, it merely repeats the same explanation, creating a loop that never reaches a foundation.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is explained by [MSK2]. [MSK2] is explained by [MSK1]. Instantiation: Eyes projecting an image is explained by a little guy watching a movie. The little guy’s perception is explained by another little guy watching a movie, which is the same as the original explanation.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because it fails to provide a genuine explanation; it simply shifts the problem to an infinite regress without proving how vision actually works.

source_article: or group in order to discredit an idea with which the person or group is associated; distracts the reader from the real issue
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: A person or group is associated with an idea. Conclusion: The idea should be discredited by attacking the person or group, distracting from the real issue.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text describes a strategy of rejecting an idea based on irrelevant characteristics of the person or group promoting it, rather than addressing the idea's merits.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is associated with [MSK2]. [MSK1] is attacked. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: The text generalizes that attacking the person or group discredits the idea.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the idea should be discredited does not follow from the premise because the attack on the person or group is irrelevant to the truth or validity of the idea itself.

source_article: Allowing overnight guests in the dorm rooms will lead to nothing but trouble: promiscuity, teen pregnancy, and eventually, a bunch of unwed mothers on campus, trying to complete their degrees while raising a child.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Allowing overnight guests in dorm rooms could lead to promiscuity. Conclusion: Allowing overnight guests will lead to teen pregnancy and unwed mothers on campus trying to complete degrees while raising a child.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that a single negative outcome (promiscuity) will inevitably lead to a chain of severe consequences (teen pregnancy, unwed mothers) without evidence that such a progression is necessary or likely.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically, slippery slope). Canonical form: If [MSK1] occurs, then [MSK2] will happen. If [MSK2] happens, then [MSK3] will happen. Therefore, [MSK1] will lead to [MSK3]. Instantiation: If overnight guests are allowed, then promiscuity will occur. If promiscuity occurs, then teen pregnancy will occur. If teen pregnancy occurs, then unwed mothers will be on campus. Therefore, allowing overnight guests will lead to unwed mothers on campus.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only asserts a potential for promiscuity, but the conclusion assumes an inevitable chain of events without supporting evidence or consideration of other factors (e.g., education, contraception).

source_article: I don’t care what scientists say, my mother always told me that the world is flat, and she would never lie to me!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My mother told me that the world is flat, and she would never lie to me. Conclusion: The world is flat.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument rejects scientific evidence in favor of a personal authority (the speaker's mother) based on trust and emotional bond, which is an appeal to personal authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] is trustworthy and never lies. Therefore, [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: [MSK1] = my mother, [MSK2] = the world is flat.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because personal trust in a non-expert does not override empirical evidence from scientists; mother's honesty does not make her claim about the Earth's shape correct.

source_article: You should eat more green leafy vegetables, because Ms. Lord says so, and she is an expert on grammar and vocabulary.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Ms. Lord says you should eat more green leafy vegetables, and she is an expert on grammar and vocabulary. Conclusion: You should eat more green leafy vegetables.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the authority of Ms. Lord, but her expertise is in grammar and vocabulary, not in nutrition or health, making the authority irrelevant to the claim.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] is an expert in [MSK3], but [MSK3] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] should be believed. Instantiation: [MSK1] = Ms. Lord, [MSK2] = you should eat more green leafy vegetables, [MSK3] = grammar and vocabulary.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because Ms. Lord's authority in grammar and vocabulary does not make her a credible source for dietary or nutritional advice; the argument relies on an irrelevant authority.

source_article: Paranormal activity is real because I've seen a ghost.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I've seen a ghost. Conclusion: Paranormal activity is real.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that seeing a ghost is evidence of paranormal activity, but the claim that ghosts exist (i.e., paranormal activity is real) is already presupposed in the premise that what was seen was a ghost.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: Paranormal activity is real because I've seen a ghost. The experience of seeing a ghost is taken as evidence, but that experience itself presupposes the reality of ghosts.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise ("I've seen a ghost") already assumes the truth of the conclusion (that paranormal activity, including ghosts, is real), so no independent evidence is provided.

source_article: My mother always told me that if I went outside right after bathing, during winter, I would catch pneumonia.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My mother told me that if I went outside right after bathing during winter, I would catch pneumonia. Conclusion: Therefore, going outside right after bathing during winter causes pneumonia.

Step 2 - Pattern: A single anecdotal instance (the mother's advice) is used to draw a general causal claim about all cases of going outside after bathing in winter.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [One anecdotal warning] has attribute [catching pneumonia]. [One anecdotal warning] is a subset of [all instances of going outside after bathing in winter]. Therefore, all [instances of going outside after bathing in winter] have attribute [catching pneumonia].

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because a single piece of advice or isolated experience is insufficient evidence to establish a general causal relationship; many other factors (e.g., health, temperature, duration) could affect the outcome.

source_article: occurs when your opponent over-simplifies or misrepresents your argument (i.e., setting up a "straw man") to make it easier to attack or refute
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The text defines a fallacy where an opponent over-simplifies or misrepresents an argument to make it easier to attack. Conclusion: This is the fallacy of extension.

Step 2 - Pattern: The text describes the pattern of setting up a "straw man" by distorting an original argument into a weaker or exaggerated version, then attacking that distorted version instead of the original.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: The opponent over-simplifies or misrepresents your argument (setting up a "straw man") to make it easier to attack or refute.

Step 4 - Verdict: The definition itself is a description of the fallacy, not an argument; thus, there is no conclusion to follow from a premise. However, the fallacy pattern it describes fails because attacking a distorted version does not refute the original claim.

source_article: We cannot support immigrants because we have too many homeless and poor Americans.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: We have too many homeless and poor Americans. Conclusion: We cannot support immigrants.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument falsely presents only two mutually exclusive options: either we help homeless and poor Americans, or we support immigrants, ignoring the possibility of doing both.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either we help homeless and poor Americans, or we support immigrants.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise does not rule out supporting immigrants while also addressing homelessness and poverty; the two are not contradictory.

source_article:
Consider the following exchange:
Omar: I think capital punishment is a necessary component of our justice system and should remain legal.
Kayla: So you are saying that murder should be legal and it is okay for us to go around killing people just because we think they deserve it? That isn't right.
Of what fallacy is Kayla guilty?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Omar claims "capital punishment is a necessary component of our justice system and should remain legal." Conclusion: Kayla attacks the idea that "murder should be legal and it is okay for us to go around killing people just because we think they deserve it."

Step 2 - Pattern: Kayla distorts Omar’s claim that capital punishment is necessary and legal into an extreme, inaccurate version that equates it to legalizing murder and arbitrary killing, then attacks that distorted version instead of addressing Omar’s actual argument.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Omar makes claim “capital punishment is a necessary component of our justice system and should remain legal.” Kayla restates this as “murder should be legal and it is okay for us to go around killing people just because we think they deserve it” (a distorted extreme). Kayla attacks the distorted version as “not right.” Therefore, Omar’s original claim is implied to be false.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Omar is advocating for murder does not follow from the premise, because capital punishment is a legally sanctioned punishment for serious crimes, not equivalent to arbitrary murder; Kayla has replaced a reasoned argument with a weaker, straw man version.

source_article: Sometimes flu vaccines don’t work, therefore vaccines are useless.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Sometimes flu vaccines don’t work. Conclusion: Vaccines are useless.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument takes a few instances where flu vaccines fail and generalizes that all vaccines are ineffective, ignoring the many cases where vaccines do work.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Some flu vaccines have the attribute of not working. Some flu vaccines are a subset of vaccines. Therefore, all vaccines have the attribute of being useless.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only refers to a few cases of flu vaccines failing, while the conclusion makes an absolute claim about all vaccines, ignoring the overwhelming evidence that most vaccines are effective.

source_article:
Timmy: Mom, what if I don’t believe in God?
Mom: Then you burn in Hell forever.  Why do you ask?
Timmy: No reason.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Timmy asks what if he doesn’t believe in God. Conclusion: He will burn in Hell forever.
Step 2 - Pattern: The mother uses the threat of eternal punishment (fear) to dissuade Timmy from doubting or questioning belief, rather than providing evidence for God’s existence or reasons to believe.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion (subtype: appeal to fear). Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: [Timmy’s disbelief being wrong] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, fear (threat of hell) is used to convince Timmy that [disbelief leads to damnation] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion (that Timmy will burn in Hell) does not follow from the premise (his question about belief) because the mother offers no logical or factual support; instead, she relies solely on instilling fear to suppress the inquiry.

source_article: "People generally like to walk on the beach. Beaches have sand. Therefore, having sand floors in homes would be a great idea!"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: People generally like to walk on the beach. Premise: Beaches have sand. Conclusion: Having sand floors in homes would be a great idea.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument draws a conclusion about a feature of homes (sand floors) based on unrelated features of beaches (sand and people liking to walk there), without establishing any relevant connection between the two contexts. This is a non sequitur.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Non sequitur. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true, and [MSK2] is true. Therefore, [MSK3] is true. Instantiation: People generally like to walk on the beach is true, and beaches have sand is true. Therefore, having sand floors in homes would be a great idea is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premises only describe features of beaches, but they provide no logical link to the desirability of sand floors in homes; the conclusion is irrelevant to the premises.

source_article: Sugar is an essential component of the body...a key material in all sorts of metabolic processes,' neglecting the fact that it is glucose (blood sugar) not ordinary table sugar (sucrose) that is the vital nourishment.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Sugar is an essential component of the body, a key material in all sorts of metabolic processes. Conclusion: Neglects the fact that it is glucose (blood sugar) not ordinary table sugar (sucrose) that is the vital nourishment.

Step 2 - Pattern: The term "sugar" is used in the premise to refer broadly to glucose (blood sugar) in the context of essential bodily functions, but in the conclusion it is implicitly shifted to refer to ordinary table sugar (sucrose), obscuring the fact that the premise's claim about essential sugar is true only for glucose, not for table sugar.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: "sugar" is used to mean glucose (blood sugar) in the premise. "sugar" is used to mean ordinary table sugar (sucrose) in the conclusion.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that sugar is essential is true only if "sugar" refers to glucose, but the argument misleadingly applies that to table sugar, which is not the same substance; the meaning of "sugar" shifts from a specific type to a different type.

source_article: Angus declares that Scotsmen do not put sugar on their porridge, to which Lachlan points out that he is a Scotsman and puts sugar on his porridge. Furious, like a true Scot, Angus yells that no true Scotsman sugars his porridge.
reasoning:
