Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Math makes no sense and it can’t be real. Conclusion: The student must not need math class.

Step 2 - Pattern: The student dismisses the validity of math based on a personal incredulity claim ("can’t be real") and uses that to justify not needing the class, showing an intent to win an argument by appealing to ignorance or personal disbelief rather than providing supporting evidence.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The student knows that math not making sense to them does not prove it is unreal. The student still claims that math is incorrect and unnecessary using an appeal to personal incredulity.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (math makes no sense to the student) is subjective and does not logically imply that math is unreal or unnecessary; the argument intentionally avoids addressing the actual evidence for math’s validity.

source_article: Atoms are colorless. Cats are made of atoms, so cats are colorless
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Atoms are colorless. Cats are made of atoms. Conclusion: Cats are colorless.
Step 2 - Pattern: This reasoning assumes that a property of individual parts (atoms being colorless) is necessarily a property of the whole composed of those parts (cats), ignoring that emergent properties can differ.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Atoms (MSK1) are colorless (MSK2). Atoms are a subset of the components of cats (MSK3). Therefore, cats (MSK3) are colorless (MSK2).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the property of being colorless applies to individual atoms, but when atoms combine to form a cat, new properties (like color) emerge that are not present in the parts alone.

source_article: Teachers claim that cell phones distract students, but they only say that because teachers have no lives.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Teachers claim that cell phones distract students. Premise: Teachers have no lives. Conclusion: Therefore, the claim that cell phones distract students is false.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the character of teachers (claiming they "have no lives") instead of addressing the merits of their claim about cell phones and distraction.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] has no lives. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Teachers are claiming that cell phones distract students. Teachers have no lives. Therefore, the claim that cell phones distract students is not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise about teachers' personal lives is irrelevant to the truth or falsity of their claim about cell phone distraction.

source_article: Look Tom, you could go to the police with your information about our questionable accounting practices here at Bentron. But remember, you have a family to feed. Besides you know how rumors of this kind of disloyalty can spread through the industry. You'll ever work as an accountant in this town again.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Tom could report questionable accounting practices, but he has a family to feed and industry rumors of disloyalty could ruin his career. Conclusion: Tom should not go to the police.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses fear of job loss and harm to his family to emotionally pressure Tom into silence, rather than providing evidence that reporting is wrong.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion (Appeal to Fear). Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: [Tom should not report the accounting practices] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, fear of losing his job and harming his family is used to convince Tom that [he should not report the accounting practices] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the threat of personal consequences does not logically justify suppressing evidence of wrongdoing; it only appeals to Tom's emotions to avoid a rational evaluation of the situation.

source_article: Every person who has ever drunk water has died. Therefore, drinking water causes death.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Every person who has ever drunk water has died. Conclusion: Therefore, drinking water causes death.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because drinking water always precedes death, it must be the cause of death, ignoring that death is inevitable from many other causes and that correlation does not imply causation.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Drinking water] occurred, then [death] occurred. Therefore, [drinking water] caused [death].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only shows a universal correlation (all who drink water die), not a causal link; death is caused by many factors (aging, disease, accidents), and water is essential for life, not a cause of death.

source_article: Deliberately using a fake issue or argument against someone because it’s easier to prove wrong than the real issue would be
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The arguer deliberately uses a fake issue or argument against someone because it’s easier to prove wrong than the real issue would be. Conclusion: This action constitutes a valid refutation of the real issue.

Step 2 - Pattern: The fallacy lies in substituting the opponent’s actual argument with a distorted, weaker version that is easier to attack, then claiming that defeating this fake version disproves the original.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2] (the real issue). [MSK3] restates [MSK2] as a fake, weaker issue. [MSK3] attacks the fake issue. Therefore, [MSK2] is considered refuted.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the real issue has been refuted does not follow from the premise, because attacking a distorted, easier-to-prove-wrong version does not address or disprove the original argument; the arguer has replaced the real issue with a straw man.

source_article: I did not complete my homework last night? Well, you did not do yours last weak either.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The speaker did not complete their homework. Conclusion: The accuser also did not do their homework last week.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker responds to a criticism about their own failure by pointing out a similar failure in the accuser, thereby attacking the accuser's character or credibility instead of addressing the original issue.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is accusing [MSK2] of [MSK3]. [MSK1] also did [MSK3]. Therefore, [MSK2]'s accusation is invalid. Instantiation: [MSK1] = "you" (the accuser), [MSK2] = "I" (the speaker), [MSK3] = "not doing homework".
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the accuser's past behavior is irrelevant to whether the speaker completed their homework; the speaker's failure stands regardless of the accuser's actions.

source_article: Either you love me, or you hate me.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: None explicitly stated. Conclusion: "Either you love me, or you hate me."
Step 2 - Pattern: The text presents only two extreme emotional options (love or hate) when there are many possible feelings, such as indifference, liking, or friendship.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you love me or you hate me is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because it falsely limits the range of possible attitudes to only two opposites, ignoring other reasonable options.

source_article: Although we have proven that the moon is not made of short ribs, we have not proven that its core cannot be filled with them; therefore, the moon’s core is filled with short ribs.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: We have proven that the moon is not made of short ribs, and we have not proven that its core cannot be filled with them. Conclusion: The moon’s core is filled with short ribs.
Step 2 - Pattern: The arguer uses a lack of disproof (not proven that the core cannot be filled with short ribs) as positive evidence for the claim, showing an intent to win the argument by shifting the burden of proof and appealing to ignorance rather than providing actual supporting evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The arguer knows that "not proven that the core cannot be filled with short ribs" does not prove the core is filled with them. The arguer still claims the moon’s core is filled with short ribs using an appeal to ignorance.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (lack of evidence against the claim) does not logically imply the claim is true; the argument intentionally uses absence of disproof as proof, which is an incorrect reasoning tactic.

source_article: Jack is a good athlete. Jack comes from Canada. Therefore, all Canadians are good athletes.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Jack is a good athlete. Jack comes from Canada. Conclusion: Therefore, all Canadians are good athletes.
Step 2 - Pattern: A single instance (Jack) is used to make a sweeping claim about an entire group (all Canadians).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Jack] has attribute [good athlete]. [Jack] is a subset of [Canadians]. Therefore, all [Canadians] have attribute [good athlete].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because one person's athletic ability does not prove that every member of a country shares that trait; the sample is too small and unrepresentative.

source_article: a mad scientist builds a rocket to the sun but plans to embark at night to avoid being cremated.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: A mad scientist builds a rocket to the sun but plans to embark at night. Conclusion: He avoids being cremated.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that traveling at night prevents the rocket from reaching the sun or that the sun's heat is absent at night, ignoring the fact that the sun is always hot regardless of Earth's night.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Non sequitur. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true, so [MSK2] is true, but [MSK1] does not logically lead to [MSK2]. Instantiation: [The scientist plans to travel at night] is true, so [he avoids being cremated] is true, but traveling at night does not logically prevent the rocket from reaching the sun's heat.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise about traveling at night is irrelevant to the sun's constant heat; the rocket would still be cremated upon reaching the sun regardless of Earth's time of day.

source_article: You should fly an American flag off your front porch. It’s the patriotic thing to do.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: It’s the patriotic thing to do. Conclusion: You should fly an American flag off your front porch.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the idea that something is correct or desirable because it aligns with the majority’s view of patriotism, without providing independent reasons.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people believe flying an American flag is the patriotic thing to do. Therefore, you should fly an American flag off your front porch.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise merely asserts a common sentiment (patriotism) without showing why that sentiment justifies the action; popularity or common belief does not make an action obligatory or correct.

source_article: Unfair judgement of people based on looks, belief, or ethnic background.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: [No explicit premise is given; the text is a definition of prejudice, not an argument]. Conclusion: [No explicit conclusion is given; the text describes a concept rather than drawing an inference].
Step 2 - Pattern: The text does not present an argument; it defines a type of unfair judgment. A faulty generalization fallacy requires an argument that moves from a specific instance or limited evidence to a broad conclusion about a group, which is absent here.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is not a faulty generalization. Canonical form: Not applicable. Instantiation: The text is a definition, not an argument with a premise and conclusion.
Step 4 - Verdict: The text does not contain an argument, so no conclusion is drawn; therefore, no fallacy can be identified.

source_article: If I make an exception for you then I'll have to make an exception for everyone
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If I make an exception for you, then I will have to make an exception for everyone. Conclusion: Therefore, I cannot make an exception for you.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that making a single exception necessarily leads to making exceptions for all similar cases, without evidence that such a chain reaction is inevitable or that the cases are truly identical in relevant respects.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically, a slippery slope subtype). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Making an exception for you (MSK1) has the attribute of requiring exceptions for everyone (MSK2). Making an exception for you is a subset of making exceptions (MSK3). Therefore, all making exceptions requires exceptions for everyone.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise assumes without justification that a single exception will inevitably lead to an unlimited cascade of exceptions, ignoring the possibility of principled distinctions or limits.

source_article: My new way of obtaining informed consent helps prevent dropouts. I chose the three therapists in our clinic who had the highest dropout rates and taught them my new informed consent procedure. The first month that they started using it, all three had significantly lower dropout rates.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I chose the three therapists in our clinic who had the highest dropout rates and taught them my new informed consent procedure. The first month that they started using it, all three had significantly lower dropout rates. Conclusion: My new way of obtaining informed consent helps prevent dropouts.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because the new procedure was introduced and then dropout rates decreased, the procedure caused the decrease, without considering other possible explanations such as regression to the mean (the therapists had unusually high dropout rates and naturally improved), or other changes in the clinic during that month.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Teaching the new informed consent procedure] occurred, then [dropout rates decreased]. Therefore, [the new procedure] caused [the decrease].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only shows a temporal correlation, not causation; the drop could be due to regression to the mean, other concurrent factors, or the placebo effect, and the sample is small and non-randomized.

source_article: We, the people, are going to work together to achieve justice, unity, and integrity.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: We, the people, are going to work together. Conclusion: This will achieve justice, unity, and integrity.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker uses emotionally charged words like "justice," "unity," and "integrity" to evoke positive feelings, rather than providing evidence or reasoning that working together will actually lead to these outcomes.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: "Working together will achieve justice, unity, and integrity" is made without evidence. In place of evidence, positive emotional associations with these ideals are used to convince the audience.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because no factual or logical support is given for how collaboration leads to justice, unity, and integrity; instead, the argument relies solely on the emotional appeal of these ideals.

source_article: Oh please, what do you know about labor laws? You don't even have a job.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: You don't even have a job. Conclusion: Therefore, what you say about labor laws is not credible.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks the person's employment status (unemployed) instead of addressing their specific claims about labor laws.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] does not have a job. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The speaker is claiming something about labor laws. The speaker does not have a job. Therefore, what the speaker claims about labor laws is not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the speaker's argument about labor laws is wrong or irrelevant does not follow from the premise that they are unemployed, because having a job is not a logical requirement for knowledge about labor laws.

source_article: Tim: Boss, you heard my side of the story why I think Bill should be fired and not me.  Now, I am sure Bill is going to come to you with some pathetic attempt to weasel out of this lie that he has created.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Bill is going to come to you with some pathetic attempt to weasel out of this lie that he has created. Conclusion: Bill's side of the story is not worth considering.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks Bill's character (calling his defense "pathetic" and accusing him of "weaseling" and lying) rather than addressing the actual content of Bill's argument.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a pathetic weasel and liar. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Bill is claiming he is not at fault. Bill is a pathetic weasel and liar. Therefore, Bill's claim is not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Bill's argument should be dismissed does not follow from the premise because attacking Bill's character does not refute the specific claims or evidence he might present.

source_article: You can’t give me a C. I’ll lose my scholarship.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I'll lose my scholarship if you give me a C. Conclusion: You cannot give me a C.
Step 2 - Pattern: The arguer uses the threat of a negative emotional outcome (losing a scholarship) to pressure the audience into accepting the conclusion, rather than providing evidence for why the grade should be higher.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion (subtype: appeal to fear). Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: "You can't give me a C" is made without evidence. In place of evidence, the fear of losing a scholarship is used to convince the listener that a C should not be given.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise only describes a potential negative consequence, not a valid reason for why the grade should be changed. The grade should be based on the quality of work, not on emotional pressure.

source_article: My opponent is an untrustworthy liar and an idiot. Therefore, you should believe me.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My opponent is an untrustworthy liar and an idiot. Conclusion: Therefore, you should believe me.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the opponent's character (untrustworthy liar, idiot) instead of addressing their claims or reasoning, and uses that attack to support a separate conclusion.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a moron. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: My opponent is claiming something. My opponent is an untrustworthy liar and an idiot. Therefore, you should believe me.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the opponent's personal traits are irrelevant to the truth of their arguments; attacking the person does not refute their claims or provide valid reason to believe the speaker instead.

source_article: "In the business meeting, she proposed a new advertising plan for the dog food brand, but I found out she actually hates dogs. How can we trust her advertising skills?"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: She proposed a new advertising plan for the dog food brand. She actually hates dogs. Conclusion: We cannot trust her advertising skills.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the person's character (hating dogs) instead of evaluating the merits of the advertising plan itself.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] hates dogs. Therefore, [MSK2] is not trustworthy. Instantiation: [She] is claiming [a new advertising plan for dog food]. [She] hates dogs. Therefore, [the advertising plan] is not trustworthy.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because hating dogs is irrelevant to the quality of her professional advertising skills; the plan should be judged on its content and effectiveness, not her personal feelings.

source_article: I guess I should buy my 12 year old daughter an iPhone. Everyone at her school has one, and I want her to fit in with the other kids.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Everyone at her school has an iPhone. Conclusion: I should buy my 12 year old daughter an iPhone so she fits in.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because a large number of people (everyone at school) own iPhones, this makes it a necessary or correct action for the daughter to have one, appealing to popularity rather than providing objective reasons.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: "Everyone at her school has an iPhone. Therefore, I should buy my daughter an iPhone."

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the popularity of iPhone ownership among peers is not a valid reason for the necessity of purchase; it ignores factors like cost, age-appropriateness, or actual need, and relies solely on social conformity.

source_article: The book Investing for Dummies really helped me understand my finances better. The book Chess for Dummies was written by the same author, was published by the same press, and costs about the same amount, so it will probably help me understand my finances, too.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The book Investing for Dummies really helped me understand my finances better. Premise: The book Chess for Dummies was written by the same author, was published by the same press, and costs about the same amount. Conclusion: The book Chess for Dummies will probably help me understand my finances, too.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because two books share superficial similarities (same author, publisher, price), they will produce the same effect (understanding finances), ignoring that the subject matter is completely different (investing vs. chess) and that the effect may depend on the content, not just those attributes.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is False Analogy. Canonical form: [MSK1] and [MSK2] share some similarities. Therefore, [MSK1] and [MSK2] will have the same effect or property. Instantiation: [Investing for Dummies] and [Chess for Dummies] share the same author, publisher, and price. Therefore, [Chess for Dummies] will also help with finances.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the relevant similarity for understanding finances is the content (investing), not the author, publisher, or price; a chess book, regardless of these similarities, is unlikely to provide financial advice.

source_article: "Right when I sneezed, the power went off. I must've caused the outage." Bragged Zohaw.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Right when I sneezed, the power went off. Conclusion: I must've caused the outage.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because sneezing and the power outage occurred at the same time, the sneeze caused the outage, ignoring that the coincidence might be random or due to another cause.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Sneezed] occurred, then [power went off] occurred. Therefore, [sneezed] caused [the power outage].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because temporal correlation alone does not establish causation; the power outage could be due to a grid failure, weather, or other factors unrelated to the sneeze.

source_article: We can have Sally as our soldier or we can have Brittany be the queen of the kingdom.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The only options for the kingdom are having Sally as a soldier or having Brittany as queen. Conclusion: Therefore, one of these two must be chosen.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text presents two mutually exclusive roles (soldier and queen) as the only possibilities, ignoring other roles or arrangements (e.g., both being soldiers, both being queens, or other individuals filling these positions).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either Sally is our soldier or Brittany is the queen.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because many other options exist (e.g., Sally could be queen, Brittany could be a soldier, both could hold different roles, or neither), so the dichotomy is false.

source_article: If we legalize marijuana, then more people will try heroin
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If we legalize marijuana, then more people will try heroin. Conclusion: Legalizing marijuana leads to heroin use.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that legalizing one drug (marijuana) will inevitably cause people to try a harder drug (heroin), based on a supposed progression from one to the other without sufficient evidence that this generalization holds for all or most cases.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] (legalizing marijuana) is associated with [MSK2] (increased heroin use) in a few instances. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3] (drug legalization). Therefore, all [MSK3] (drug legalization) leads to [MSK2] (increased heroin use). Instantiation: Legalizing marijuana is assumed to cause more heroin use, generalizing from a few cases or a slippery slope assumption.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise provides no evidence that marijuana legalization causally leads to heroin use; it is a hasty generalization based on a flawed assumption of a causal chain.

source_article:
In her paper, Aida states "Filthy, polluting hummers should be banned."
Name that fallacy:
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Aida states "Filthy, polluting hummers should be banned." Conclusion: The fallacy in this statement is circular reasoning.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text presents a claim from a paper but does not provide any reasoning or evidence; it simply asserts a conclusion without any supporting premises that would create a circular argument.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: The text does not match the canonical form because it lacks a premise-conclusion structure; it is merely an assertion without circular justification.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the text does not contain an argument with premises and a conclusion that circle back; it is just a statement, so no fallacy of circular reasoning is present.

source_article: The secretary claims that mining is doing more harm than good to the environment and to the people. The pro-mining politicians accused her of being anti-poor who values the life of a tree more than the life of miners whose only source of income is mining.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The secretary claims that mining is doing more harm than good to the environment and to the people. Conclusion: The pro-mining politicians accuse her of being anti-poor who values the life of a tree more than the life of miners whose only source of income is mining.

Step 2 - Pattern: The pro-mining politicians distort the secretary’s claim about mining’s overall harm (including both environmental and social impacts) into a false dichotomy that she cares more about trees than miners, then attack this distorted version as being anti-poor.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: The secretary makes claim “mining is doing more harm than good to the environment and to the people.” Pro-mining politicians restate this as “she values the life of a tree more than the life of miners” (a distorted extreme that ignores the original nuance). They attack the distorted version as being anti-poor. Therefore, the original claim is implied to be false.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the secretary is anti-poor does not follow from the premise, because her claim about mining’s overall harm does not imply she prioritizes trees over miners; the politicians have replaced her balanced criticism with a weaker, exaggerated version and attacked that instead.

source_article: Famous actors like Alex Gonzaga and Philip Salvador support Oplan Tokhang, so it must be an effective operation.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Famous actors like Alex Gonzaga and Philip Salvador support Oplan Tokhang. Conclusion: Oplan Tokhang must be an effective operation.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the endorsement of celebrities (actors) who lack relevant expertise in law enforcement or crime prevention, relying on their fame rather than credible authority, which is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1], who are not experts in [MSK2], claim [MSK2] is effective. Therefore, [MSK2] is effective. Instantiation: [MSK1] = famous actors (Alex Gonzaga, Philip Salvador), [MSK2] = Oplan Tokhang.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the support of celebrities does not provide evidence of the operation's effectiveness; their fame does not qualify them to make credible judgments about law enforcement policies.

source_article: AttP 8: everyone having strict controls on firearms except the U.S.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Everyone (the majority of countries) has strict controls on firearms except the U.S. Conclusion: Therefore, the U.S. should adopt strict controls on firearms.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the fact that many other countries have strict firearm controls, implying that because it is a widespread practice, it must be correct or beneficial for the U.S. to follow.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of countries have strict controls on firearms. Therefore, strict controls on firearms must be the right policy.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the popularity of a policy does not guarantee its correctness or suitability for a different context; the U.S. may have unique circumstances that justify different firearm regulations.

source_article:
All stars are exploding balls of gas.
Miley Cyrus is a star.
Therefore, Miley Cyrus is an exploding ball of gas
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: All stars are exploding balls of gas. Miley Cyrus is a star. Conclusion: Miley Cyrus is an exploding ball of gas.
Step 2 - Pattern: The word "star" in the first premise refers to a celestial astronomical body (a massive, luminous sphere of plasma), while in the second premise it refers to a famous celebrity, shifting the meaning from an astronomical object to a human entertainer.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: "star" is used to mean a celestial body in the first premise. "star" is used to mean a celebrity in the second premise and conclusion.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that all celestial stars are exploding balls of gas does not apply to Miley Cyrus, who is a celebrity "star," not an astronomical star; the meaning of "star" shifts between the premises and the conclusion.

source_article: Which fallacy literally means "He did it himself?"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The question asks which fallacy literally means "He did it himself?". Conclusion: The fallacy is identified as "ipse dixit".
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument relies on a literal translation of a Latin phrase ("He did it himself") to label a fallacy, which is a form of appeal to authority or credibility because it assumes that the literal meaning of the term itself provides the correct classification, rather than evaluating the argument's logical structure.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] literally means [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK1] is the correct label for the fallacy. Instantiation: [MSK1] = "ipse dixit", [MSK2] = "He did it himself".
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the literal translation of a term does not guarantee that it is the correct fallacy label; the fallacy "ipse dixit" refers to an unsupported assertion of authority, not merely the phrase's etymology.

source_article: "Everytime I wash my car it rains; therefore, washing my car makes it rain."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Everytime I wash my car it rains. Conclusion: Washing my car makes it rain.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because one event (washing the car) consistently precedes another event (rain), the first event must cause the second, ignoring the possibility of coincidence, unrelated weather patterns, or other factors.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Washing my car] occurred, then [it rains]. Therefore, [washing my car] caused [it to rain].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only states a temporal correlation, not a causal mechanism; rain is caused by meteorological conditions, not by car washing, and the correlation is likely due to chance or confirmation bias.

source_article:
Pamela says that she thinks that the class should do more service projects. Mark says he can't believe that Pamela doesn't support the annual school dance.
Pamela dice que ella piensa que la clase debería hacer más proyectos de servicio. Mark dice que no puede creer que Pamela no apoye el baile escolar anual
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Pamela says she thinks the class should do more service projects. Conclusion: Mark says he can't believe that Pamela doesn't support the annual school dance.

Step 2 - Pattern: Mark misrepresents Pamela's claim about supporting more service projects as a position against the school dance, then attacks that distorted position as if it were Pamela's original argument.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Pamela makes claim "the class should do more service projects." Mark restates this as "Pamela doesn't support the annual school dance" (distorted). Mark attacks the distorted version by expressing disbelief. Therefore, Pamela's original claim is implied to be unreasonable.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Pamela doesn't support the school dance does not follow from the premise, because advocating for more service projects does not imply opposition to the dance; Mark has replaced Pamela's original argument with a weaker, distorted version.

source_article: Christina's shoes are superior, because they're made from one hundred percent snakeskin, not synthetic materials.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Christina's shoes are made from one hundred percent snakeskin, not synthetic materials. Conclusion: Christina's shoes are superior.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the natural origin (snakeskin) versus synthetic materials as a basis for claiming superiority, which is an appeal to nature, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made from natural material [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK1] is superior to synthetic alternatives. Instantiation: [MSK1] = Christina's shoes, [MSK2] = snakeskin.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because naturalness does not automatically imply superiority; synthetic materials can be more durable, ethical, or effective, and no evidence is given that snakeskin makes the shoes superior in any specific aspect.

source_article: "Do the best for your baby"  is an example of ...
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: No explicit premise is stated; the phrase "Do the best for your baby" is presented as an example. Conclusion: The implied conclusion is that the audience should follow the advice or product being promoted.
Step 2 - Pattern: The phrase manipulates the audience's emotions—specifically their love and concern for their baby—to persuade them to accept a course of action, without providing factual evidence or logical reasoning.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: [MSK1] = "You should follow the advice/purchase the product" is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion (parental love and concern) is used to convince the interlocutor that this is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that one should act a certain way does not follow from the premise, because the phrase only triggers emotional response rather than providing rational support for the action.

source_article: When Squealer/Napoleon calls Snowball a 'traitor', and 'criminal'
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Squealer/Napoleon calls Snowball a "traitor" and "criminal". Conclusion: Snowball's arguments or actions are invalid or wrong.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks Snowball's character by labeling him a "traitor" and "criminal" instead of addressing his actual claims or actions.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a traitor/criminal. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Squealer/Napoleon is claiming that Snowball's positions are wrong. Snowball is called a traitor and criminal. Therefore, Snowball's positions are not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Snowball's arguments are invalid does not follow from the premise that he is called a "traitor" or "criminal," because his character is irrelevant to the truth or falsity of his claims.

source_article: Don't you want the best for your baby?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: [Implicit: The speaker suggests that the listener wants the best for their baby.]. Conclusion: [The listener should agree with the speaker’s implied point or action].
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument bypasses logical reasoning and directly appeals to the listener's emotional desire to care for their child, using that emotion to pressure agreement without providing evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: [The implied claim that the speaker’s position is correct] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, the emotion of parental love and concern is used to convince the interlocutor that [the speaker’s position] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the listener should agree with the speaker does not follow from the premise because the desire to do what is best for one’s baby does not logically compel acceptance of the speaker’s unstated claim; the argument manipulates emotion rather than providing rational support.

source_article: Fatima sneezed and she caused a tsunami in Tokyo.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Fatima sneezed and she caused a tsunami in Tokyo. Conclusion: Fatima's sneeze caused a tsunami in Tokyo.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because two events occurred (a sneeze and a tsunami), the first event caused the second, without any plausible causal mechanism or supporting evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Fatima sneezing] occurred, then [a tsunami in Tokyo] occurred. Therefore, [Fatima sneezing] caused [the tsunami].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because a sneeze is far too small and localized to cause a tsunami; the temporal sequence is coincidental and lacks any causal link, making the inference baseless.

source_article: You can either start using plastic straws or continue killing ocean life with plastic pollution.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "You can either start using plastic straws or continue killing ocean life with plastic pollution." Conclusion: These are the only two options regarding plastic straw use and ocean life.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text presents a binary choice between using plastic straws and harming ocean life, ignoring other possibilities such as using alternatives (e.g., paper, metal, or reusable straws) or reducing other plastic pollution sources.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either [start using plastic straws] or [continue killing ocean life with plastic pollution] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise falsely restricts the options; one could avoid plastic straws without causing ocean harm by using eco-friendly alternatives, or address plastic pollution through other means.

source_article: the best money can buy…  (Don’t mention it’s expensive and often needs repair.)
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The product is described as "the best money can buy". Conclusion: The product is excellent and worth the cost, without mentioning it is expensive and often needs repair.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker intentionally omits negative information (expense, frequent repairs) that would undermine the claim of being the best, showing an intent to win the argument by hiding information rather than providing full supporting evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows "the best money can buy" is incorrect because the product is expensive and often needs repair. The speaker still claims it is correct using an incorrect argument (hiding the negative facts).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise omits crucial counterevidence; the argument intentionally suppresses information to make an unsupported claim.

source_article: Mr. Casal is a bad teacher because he stinks.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Mr. Casal stinks. Conclusion: Mr. Casal is a bad teacher.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks a personal characteristic (body odor) of Mr. Casal rather than addressing his teaching abilities or performance.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] stinks. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Mr. Casal is claiming to be a teacher. Mr. Casal stinks. Therefore, Mr. Casal is a bad teacher.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise about body odor is irrelevant to judging teaching quality; teaching skill is not determined by personal hygiene.

source_article: Jill and Jane have some concerns that the rules their sorority has set are racist in character. Since Jill is a decent person, she brings her concerns up in the next meeting. The president of the sorority assures her that there is nothing wrong with the rules, since the majority of the sisters like them. Jane accepts this ruling but Jill decides to leave the sorority.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The majority of the sisters like the rules. Conclusion: There is nothing wrong with the rules (i.e., they are not racist).
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument asserts that because most members approve of the rules, the rules are correct or acceptable, ignoring the possibility that the majority could be mistaken or biased.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A majority of the sisters like the rules. Therefore, the rules are not racist.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the popularity of a belief does not guarantee its truth; the majority's approval could stem from ignorance, prejudice, or conformity, not from the rules being non-racist.

source_article: "I had a bad teacher once; therefore, all teachers must be bad."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I had a bad teacher once. Conclusion: All teachers must be bad.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument draws a universal conclusion about all teachers based on a single instance of a bad teacher, ignoring the vast number of other teachers who may not be bad.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: That one bad teacher has the attribute of being bad. That one bad teacher is a subset of all teachers. Therefore, all teachers have the attribute of being bad.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that all teachers are bad does not follow from the premise that one teacher was bad, because a single instance is insufficient to establish a universal property for an entire group.

source_article: Kevin's dog scratched his leg, and that night he had a fever. Kevin concluded that his dog must have infected him with something.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Kevin's dog scratched his leg, and that night he had a fever. Conclusion: Kevin concluded that his dog must have infected him with something.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because the dog scratch preceded the fever, the scratch must have caused the infection, ignoring other possible causes for the fever.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [The dog scratched his leg] occurred, then [he had a fever]. Therefore, [the dog] caused [the infection].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the fever could be due to an unrelated illness, and the temporal sequence alone does not establish a causal link between the scratch and the infection.

source_article: There are more laws on the books than ever before, and more crimes are being committed than ever before. Therefore, to reduce crime, we must eliminate the laws.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: There are more laws on the books than ever before, and more crimes are being committed than ever before. Conclusion: Therefore, to reduce crime, we must eliminate the laws.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because two trends (increase in laws and increase in crime) occur together, one must cause the other, and that reversing the first (eliminating laws) will reverse the second, without considering that the correlation might be coincidental or that other factors (e.g., population growth, reporting changes) could explain the increase in crime.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [More laws] occurred, then [more crimes] occurred. Therefore, [more laws] caused [more crimes].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only shows a correlation, not a causal link; eliminating laws might not reduce crime if crime is driven by other factors, and the increase in crime could be due to unrelated causes.

source_article: Men score better on math than women do. Jerry is a man. Therefore, Jerry is better at math than Sylvia, who is a woman.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Men score better on math than women do. Jerry is a man. Conclusion: Therefore, Jerry is better at math than Sylvia, who is a woman.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that a statistical average about a group applies to every individual member of that group, ignoring variation within the group.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (subtype: Accident). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Men] have attribute [score better on math than women]. [Men] is a subset of [people]. Therefore, [all people who are men] have attribute [score better at math than all people who are women].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only states a general average difference between groups, not a guarantee about any specific individual (Jerry and Sylvia). Jerry might be worse at math than Sylvia, despite the group trend.

source_article: Misrepresenting someone’s argument to make it easier to attack
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Someone misrepresents someone else’s argument to make it easier to attack. Conclusion: The misrepresentation is a fallacy of extension.

Step 2 - Pattern: The text describes the act of distorting an original argument into a weaker or exaggerated version, then attacking that distorted version instead of the original, which is the core of the straw man fallacy.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] in a distorted way to make it easier to attack. [MSK3] attacks the distorted version. Therefore, [MSK2] is false.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the argument is fallacious does not follow from the premise, because the text merely defines the fallacy without providing a specific argument to evaluate; the definition itself is correct but does not constitute an argument that fails to follow.

source_article: "You know me - I've taught Sunday School at your church for years, babysat your children, and served as a playground director for many summers - so you know I can run your preschool."?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The speaker has taught Sunday School at your church for years, babysat your children, and served as a playground director for many summers. Conclusion: The speaker can run your preschool.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument relies on the speaker's personal history and trusted community role (Sunday School teacher, babysitter, playground director) as direct evidence of qualification to run a preschool, which is an appeal to personal credibility or authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2] is qualified to [MSK3] because [MSK1] has performed [MSK4] roles. Therefore, [MSK2] should be accepted. Instantiation: [MSK1] = the speaker, [MSK2] = the speaker, [MSK3] = run a preschool, [MSK4] = Sunday School teacher, babysitter, playground director.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because being a trusted community figure in related but different roles (Sunday School, babysitting, playground supervision) does not automatically qualify someone to run a preschool, which requires specific administrative, educational, and regulatory expertise not demonstrated in the premise.

source_article: men don’t cry
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Some men do not cry (or an observed instance). Conclusion: All men do not cry.
Step 2 - Pattern: The fallacy draws a universal claim about all men based on a limited or stereotypical observation of some men.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Some men do not cry. Some men are a subset of all men. Therefore, all men do not cry.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise describes only a subset or stereotype, not evidence for every individual man, ignoring variation in emotional expression.

source_article: A real estate ad shows a happy family moving in to the home of their dreams.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The ad shows a happy family moving into their dream home. Conclusion: The home is desirable and should be purchased.
Step 2 - Pattern: The ad uses an image of a happy family to evoke positive emotions, such as joy and aspiration, rather than providing factual evidence about the home's features, price, or location.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: The claim that the home is desirable is made without evidence about its quality or value. In place of evidence, the image of a happy family is used to evoke positive feelings.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the home is desirable does not follow from the premise because the happy family image is an emotional appeal, not a logical reason to purchase the home.

source_article: God did not create the world six thousand years ago because matter has always existed, and therefore the world has always existed.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "Matter has always existed, and therefore the world has always existed." Conclusion: "God did not create the world six thousand years ago."
Step 2 - Pattern: The conclusion that God did not create the world is supported by the premise that matter and the world have always existed, but that premise itself assumes the very point at issue—that the world was not created (by God or otherwise).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: "God did not create the world" is true because "the world has always existed". "The world has always existed" is true because "matter has always existed", but "matter has always existed" is used to deny creation, thus assuming the conclusion.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that matter and the world have always existed is not independently established; it is merely a restatement of the denial of creation, so the argument moves in a circle without providing evidence.

source_article: It's common sense that if you smack your children, they will stop the bad behavior. So don't tell me not to hit my kids.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: It's common sense that if you smack your children, they will stop the bad behavior. Conclusion: Don't tell me not to hit my kids.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker asserts "common sense" as an appeal to an unproven, assumed truth, showing an intent to win the argument by dismissing opposing evidence or reasoning without providing actual supporting evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows that "common sense" does not prove smacking stops bad behavior, yet still claims that hitting kids is justified using an appeal to common sense (an incorrect argument).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise relies on an unsubstantiated appeal to "common sense" rather than empirical evidence, intentionally avoiding the need for proof.

source_article:
BILL CLINTON: But when Joe McCarthy went around this country attacking people’s patriotism, he was wrong. He was wrong. And a senator from Connecticut stood up to him named Prescott Bush. Your father was right to stand up to Joe McCarthy, you were wrong to attack my patriotism. I was opposed to the war but I loved my country… Debate 1,
October 11, 1992
St. Louis, Missouri
What logical fallacy did Clinton’s statement employ?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: George H.W. Bush attacked Bill Clinton's patriotism. Conclusion: Therefore, George H.W. Bush's criticism of Clinton's patriotism is wrong.
Step 2 - Pattern: Clinton attacks the person (Bush) by accusing him of repeating Joe McCarthy's tactics, rather than addressing the substance of Bush's claim about Clinton's patriotism.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem (tu quoque subtype). Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a hypocrite. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: George H.W. Bush is claiming Clinton is unpatriotic. Bush is a hypocrite (because his father opposed McCarthy, and Bush is now doing something similar). Therefore, Bush's claim is false.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Bush's attack on Clinton's patriotism is wrong does not follow from the premise that Bush's father opposed McCarthy; Bush's personal history or alleged hypocrisy does not address the truth or falsity of his claim about Clinton's patriotism.

source_article: "Every time I go to sleep the sun goes down. Therefore, my sleeping causes the sun to set."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Every time I go to sleep the sun goes down. Conclusion: My sleeping causes the sun to set.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument infers a causal relationship solely from a repeated temporal sequence, assuming that because one event consistently follows another, the first must be the cause of the second.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [I go to sleep] occurred, then [the sun goes down]. Therefore, [my sleeping] caused [the sun to set].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only shows a correlation in time, not a causal link; the sun setting is due to Earth's rotation, not the speaker's sleep, and the temporal order is coincidental.

source_article: If I don't get this 5 point assignment done then I will fall behind in my grade and will get backed up so I can't get my other assignment done. Then I will fail the class and turn to a life of crime.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Not completing a single 5-point assignment leads to falling behind, then failing the class. Conclusion: Therefore, not completing the assignment will cause a life of crime.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker takes a minor setback (one missed assignment) and projects an extreme, unlikely chain of events (failing the class and turning to crime) without evidence that each step necessarily follows.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically, Slippery Slope). Canonical form: Event A leads to B, B leads to C, C leads to D, therefore A leads to D without justification that each link is inevitable. Instantiation: Not doing one assignment leads to falling behind, which leads to failing the class, which leads to a life of crime.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only describes a possible outcome of one missed assignment, but the leap to a life of crime is unsupported and ignores other factors (e.g., catching up, seeking help, alternative consequences).

source_article: Presents only two choices when more options exist
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: [The text states that only two choices are presented.] Conclusion: [Therefore, the argument is a false dilemma.]
Step 2 - Pattern: The text describes the very definition of a false dilemma: presenting only two options when more exist, which is the core pattern of this fallacy.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: The text itself is the instantiation, describing the fallacy rather than committing it.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the text is a definition, not an argument; it correctly identifies the fallacy pattern without making a false claim about limited options.

source_article: If we keep kids from enjoying recess, eventually they will never want to preserve nature.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If we keep kids from enjoying recess, they will eventually never want to preserve nature. Conclusion: A specific restriction (keeping kids from recess) leads to an extreme outcome (never wanting to preserve nature).
Step 2 - Pattern: This is a slippery slope fallacy, a subtype of faulty generalization, where a minor initial step is claimed to lead inevitably to a drastic and unrelated consequence without evidence for the causal chain.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is a faulty generalization (slippery slope). Canonical form: If [MSK1] occurs, then [MSK2] will happen, and then [MSK3] will follow, leading to [extreme outcome]. Instantiation: If kids are kept from recess, then they will lose enjoyment of outdoor play, and then they will never want to preserve nature.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise assumes a direct causal link between denying recess and a complete loss of desire to preserve nature, ignoring other factors and intermediate steps, and overgeneralizing from a single action to an extreme, unsupported outcome.

source_article: What’s the point of making drinking illegal under the age of 21?  Kids still manage to get alcohol.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Kids still manage to get alcohol despite the drinking age being 21. Conclusion: There’s no point in making drinking illegal under the age of 21.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker uses the fact that the law is imperfectly enforced (kids still get alcohol) to argue that the law is pointless, showing an intent to win the argument by cherry-picking a failure without considering the law’s deterrent or regulatory effects.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows that “kids still manage to get alcohol” does not prove the law is pointless. The speaker still claims the law is pointless using an incorrect argument (ignoring the law’s other benefits).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (kids still get alcohol) only shows the law is not fully effective, not that it has no point; the argument intentionally ignores evidence that the law reduces overall underage drinking and its harms.

source_article: "Cars cause more deaths than firearms do, so if we ban firearms we should also ban cars."
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Cars cause more deaths than firearms do. Premise: If we ban firearms we should also ban cars. Conclusion: The speaker implies that banning firearms logically requires banning cars.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument draws a false analogy between firearms and cars, ignoring relevant differences such as purpose, regulation, and societal benefit, and falsely assumes that because both cause deaths, they should be treated identically.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is False Analogy. Canonical form: [MSK1] and [MSK2] are similar in one respect, so they must be similar in all respects. Instantiation: Cars and firearms both cause deaths, so if we ban one we must ban the other.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the analogy overlooks critical differences—cars are primarily for transportation with safety regulations, while firearms are weapons often used for self-defense or recreation—so the same policy does not logically apply.

source_article: "You will never be satisfied in life if you don’t seize this opportunity. Do you want to live the rest of your years yearning to know what would have happened if you just jumped when you had the chance?" Which appeal is the primary appeal used in this quote?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: You will never be satisfied in life if you don’t seize this opportunity. Do you want to live the rest of your years yearning to know what would have happened if you just jumped when you had the chance? Conclusion: You should seize the opportunity.

Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attempts to persuade by evoking fear of future regret and dissatisfaction, rather than providing rational evidence for why seizing the opportunity is beneficial.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: [Seizing the opportunity] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion (fear of regret and dissatisfaction) is used to convince the interlocutor that [seizing the opportunity] is true.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise only invokes emotional consequences (dissatisfaction and regret) without providing any factual or logical support for why seizing the opportunity is actually beneficial or necessary.

source_article: SM 3: Polls concerning global warming
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Polls show that a lot of people believe in global warming. Conclusion: Global warming must be true.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument relies on the popularity of a belief as evidence for its truth, rather than on empirical data or scientific reasoning about global warming itself.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people believe global warming is real. Therefore, global warming must be true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the truth of global warming is determined by scientific evidence, not by the number of people who believe in it.

source_article: Everybody loves Raymond because he is so popular. Popular people are loved by everybody.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "Everybody loves Raymond because he is so popular." Conclusion: "Popular people are loved by everybody."
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses "Raymond is popular" to prove "everybody loves Raymond," and then uses "everybody loves Raymond" to prove "popular people are loved by everybody," but the second statement essentially restates the first without independent evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: "Everybody loves Raymond" is true because "he is so popular." "Popular people are loved by everybody" is true because "everybody loves Raymond."
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that "popular people are loved by everybody" does not follow from the premise because it merely rephrases the original claim that "everybody loves Raymond" without providing any new evidence or reasoning to support the connection between popularity and being loved.

source_article: Everyone does it.  You should too.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Everyone does it. Conclusion: You should too.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument claims that because a large number of people (everyone) engage in a behavior, it is therefore a good or correct action for you to follow.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people do it. Therefore, doing it must be correct.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because popularity does not guarantee correctness, morality, or appropriateness; the action could be harmful, unethical, or irrelevant to the individual's situation.

source_article: "She's such a mean teacher and person – that's why she wouldn't raise my grade ONE percent!"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "She's such a mean teacher and person." Conclusion: "That's why she wouldn't raise my grade one percent."
Step 2 - Pattern: The arguer attacks the teacher's character (calling her mean) instead of addressing whether the grade was fair or whether there was a valid reason not to raise it.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a moron. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The teacher is claiming the grade should not be raised. The teacher is a mean person. Therefore, the grade should not be raised is not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the teacher's personality (being mean) has no logical bearing on the correctness of the grade or the decision not to raise it.

source_article: The officer told me to freeze but it was too hot out to be freezing, so I was justified in running away.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The officer told me to freeze but it was too hot out to be freezing. Conclusion: I was justified in running away.

Step 2 - Pattern: The word "freeze" in the premise is used in two different senses: first as a police command meaning "stop and remain still," and second as a literal temperature condition meaning "to become ice-cold." The speaker equivocates between these meanings to avoid complying with the command.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: "freeze" is used to mean a police command to stop in the premise. "freeze" is used to mean the literal process of becoming ice-cold in the justification for running away.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that it was too hot to freeze literally does not negate the police command to stop; the meaning of "freeze" shifts from a command to a temperature condition, so the officer's order remains valid.

source_article:
All boys are sports fans.
Some bakers are boys.
Therefore, some bakers are not sports fans.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: All boys are sports fans. Premise: Some bakers are boys. Conclusion: Therefore, some bakers are not sports fans.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument incorrectly infers that because some bakers are boys and all boys are sports fans, then some bakers must not be sports fans. This ignores the possibility that all bakers could be sports fans, as the premises do not exclude that scenario.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Non sequitur. Canonical form: [MSK1] and [MSK2] are true, therefore [MSK3] is true, but [MSK3] does not follow logically from [MSK1] and [MSK2]. Instantiation: All boys are sports fans and some bakers are boys are true, therefore some bakers are not sports fans is true, but this conclusion does not follow because the premises allow that all bakers (including those who are boys) could be sports fans.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because from "all boys are sports fans" and "some bakers are boys," we can only deduce that some bakers are sports fans (the ones who are boys), but we cannot deduce that some bakers are not sports fans, as the premises do not rule out that all bakers are sports fans.

source_article: You can buy the new Sensitivity perfume, or you can smell bad all day.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: You can buy the new Sensitivity perfume, or you can smell bad all day. Conclusion: These are the only two options for how you smell.

Step 2 - Pattern: The text presents only two possibilities—buying the perfume or smelling bad—while ignoring other alternatives, such as using a different perfume, using no perfume but still smelling neutral, or other hygiene products.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you buy the new Sensitivity perfume, or you smell bad all day.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that these are the only two options does not follow because there are many other ways to smell pleasant or neutral without buying this specific perfume, so the premise falsely limits the choices.

source_article: Appealing to popularity or the fact that many people do something as an attempted form of validation
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Many people do something or hold a belief. Conclusion: That something is valid or true.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument claims that the mere popularity of an idea or action is sufficient evidence for its correctness, ignoring that truth is not determined by majority opinion.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: Many people do something; therefore, that action is validated.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because popularity does not guarantee truth or validity; a belief or action can be widely held yet false or wrong.

source_article: “Police officers agree that Blackjack Steering Wheel Locks are the most effective deterrent against car theft.”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Police officers agree that Blackjack Steering Wheel Locks are the most effective deterrent against car theft. Conclusion: Blackjack Steering Wheel Locks are the most effective deterrent against car theft.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the authority of police officers to support the product's effectiveness, without providing evidence that police officers have relevant expertise in evaluating car theft deterrents or that their agreement is based on impartial testing.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2] is the most effective. [MSK1] are authorities. Therefore, [MSK2] is the most effective. Instantiation: [MSK1] = police officers, [MSK2] = Blackjack Steering Wheel Locks.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because police officers may not have specialized knowledge about all steering wheel locks or may be influenced by marketing, and their agreement does not constitute objective evidence of effectiveness.

source_article: Mr. Casal was very tired because he had no energy.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Mr. Casal had no energy. Conclusion: Mr. Casal was very tired.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument defines "being tired" as equivalent to "having no energy," so the conclusion merely restates the premise without providing independent evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: Mr. Casal was very tired because he had no energy. He had no energy because he was very tired.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise and conclusion are synonymous; no new information is added to support the claim.

source_article: I like my steak rare. But I don’t care for aardvark steak, even though it’s the rarest steak in the world.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I like my steak rare. Conclusion: I don’t care for aardvark steak, even though it’s the rarest steak in the world.

Step 2 - Pattern: The word "rare" is used in the premise to mean a degree of doneness in cooking (lightly cooked), while in the conclusion "rarest" is used to mean uncommon or scarce in availability, shifting from a culinary term to a statistical or frequency term.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is equivocation. Canonical form: [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK2] in the premise. [MSK1] is used to mean [MSK3] in the conclusion. Instantiation: "rare" is used to mean lightly cooked in the premise. "rarest" is used to mean most uncommon in the conclusion.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise that the speaker likes steak cooked rare does not imply anything about whether they would like steak that is uncommon; the meaning of "rare" shifts from a cooking preference to a description of scarcity.

source_article: Habib didn't remember to take out the trash this morning. It will pile up and the neighbors will complain and the health inspectors will come and take away the kids.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Habib didn't remember to take out the trash this morning. Conclusion: It will pile up and the neighbors will complain and the health inspectors will come and take away the kids.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that a single failure to take out the trash will inevitably lead to a catastrophic chain of events (trash piling up, neighbors complaining, health inspectors taking away children), without evidence that such a chain will occur or is typical.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: This text does not fit the canonical form well; instead, it is a hasty generalization where one instance (forgetting trash) is generalized to an extreme, unlikely outcome (health inspectors taking kids).
