The pattern is that a single event is projected to cause a whole series of improbable consequences.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because forgetting the trash once does not logically lead to trash piling up uncontrollably, neighbors complaining, or health inspectors taking children; it ignores normal mitigating factors (e.g., the trash can be taken out later, neighbors may not complain, health inspectors are unlikely to intervene over one missed trash day).

source_article: The music industry must either raise album prices or reduce pay for technicians and producers.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The music industry faces a choice between raising album prices or reducing pay for technicians and producers. Conclusion: These are the only viable options.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text presents two alternatives as if they are the only possibilities, ignoring other potential solutions like increasing sales volume, cutting executive bonuses, or using cheaper distribution methods.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either raise album prices or reduce pay for technicians and producers is the only option.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because many other strategies exist (e.g., expanding digital revenue, reducing marketing costs, or renegotiating contracts) that are not considered, making the dichotomy false.

source_article: Primitive people believed in gods to explain natural phenomena. We have science, and are not primitive anymore. Therefore, there is no God.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Primitive people believed in gods to explain natural phenomena. We have science, and are not primitive anymore. Conclusion: Therefore, there is no God.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks believers (primitive people) rather than addressing the existence of God directly, implying that because primitive people believed in God, the belief is false now that we are not primitive.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem (specifically genetic fallacy). Canonical form: [MSK1] (primitive people) believed in [MSK2] (God). [MSK1] are primitive. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Primitive people believed in God. They are primitive. Therefore, God does not exist.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion about God's existence does not follow from the premise about primitive beliefs; the origin of a belief does not determine its truth or falsity.

source_article: Either you can save your money to buy a new car for yourself, or you can just take an Uber around for the rest of your life.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: You have two options: save money to buy a new car or take Uber for the rest of your life. Conclusion: You must choose one of these two options.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker presents only two alternatives (saving for a car or taking Uber forever) as if they are the only possible choices, ignoring other options like using public transportation, biking, carpooling, or using ride-sharing services occasionally.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you save your money to buy a new car for yourself, or you just take an Uber around for the rest of your life.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because many other transportation options exist (e.g., public transit, biking, walking, carpooling, or using Uber only occasionally) that are omitted, making the presented binary choice false.

source_article: Why did the blond cross the road?  Because she saw a shoe sale!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: One blond individual crossed the road because she saw a shoe sale. Conclusion: All blonds cross the road because they see shoe sales.
Step 2 - Pattern: A single instance (one blond's action) is used to draw a sweeping conclusion about an entire group (all blonds), ignoring the diversity of individual behavior.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [one blond] has attribute [crossed the road for a shoe sale]. [one blond] is a subset of [all blonds]. Therefore, all blonds have attribute [cross the road for shoe sales].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only describes a single, possibly atypical, instance; there is no evidence that this behavior is characteristic of all blonds, and the generalization is unwarranted.

source_article:
You cannot blame your friends for a rain delay just because every time they go with you to a ballgame it storms and
play is delayed. Likewise, the fact that a pitcher bought new socks before he pitched a winning game does not mean that new
socks cause a pitcher to throw faster.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: You cannot blame your friends for a rain delay just because every time they go to a ballgame it storms and play is delayed; likewise, the fact that a pitcher bought new socks before he pitched a winning game does not mean that new socks cause a pitcher to throw faster. Conclusion: (implied) The examples illustrate that correlation or temporal sequence does not prove causation.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text provides two counterexamples to the false causality fallacy, showing that assuming a causal relationship from mere coincidence or temporal order is mistaken; it points out the fallacy in reasoning without committing it.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Friends go to a ballgame] occurred, then [rain and delay] occurred. Therefore, [friends going] caused [rain delay]. And [Pitcher bought new socks] occurred, then [pitcher won game] occurred. Therefore, [new socks] caused [winning].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the text is an example of false causality does not follow because the text actually refutes the fallacy, not commits it; it argues that such reasoning is invalid.

source_article: The phrase, "Almost everyone that was asked said that McDonalds is better than Burger King, so it must be true," represents which fallacy?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Almost everyone that was asked said that McDonalds is better than Burger King. Conclusion: So it must be true that McDonalds is better than Burger King.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that the popularity of an opinion (the majority's belief) is sufficient evidence for its truth, without any supporting factual or logical justification.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people believe McDonalds is better than Burger King. Therefore, McDonalds being better than Burger King must be true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the majority's belief does not establish objective truth; taste or quality judgments require evidence beyond mere popularity.

source_article: The school is in bad shape. Either we tear it down and put up a new building, or we continue to risk students’ safety. Obviously, we shouldn’t risk anyone’s safety, so we must tear the building down.”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The school is in bad shape. Conclusion: We must tear down the building.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument presents only two options—tearing down the building or risking students’ safety—ignoring other possibilities like renovating, reinforcing, or temporarily relocating students.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either we tear it down and put up a new building, or we continue to risk students’ safety.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise does not exclude intermediate solutions; there are many ways to address the school’s poor condition without tearing it down.

source_article: Employees are like nails. Just as nails must be hit in the head in order to make them work, so must employees.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Employees are like nails. Premise: Nails must be hit in the head to make them work. Conclusion: Employees must be hit in the head to make them work.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument draws a conclusion about employees based on a superficial similarity to nails, ignoring that the analogy fails because the properties of nails (being hit in the head) do not logically transfer to employees (who are not inanimate objects).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is False Analogy. Canonical form: [MSK1] and [MSK2] are similar in some respects, therefore they are similar in the respect claimed. Instantiation: [Nails] and [employees] are similar in that both are used for work, therefore [employees] must be hit in the head to work, just as [nails] are hit in the head to work.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the analogy is weak and irrelevant; the way nails function (being struck) has no logical bearing on how employees should be treated, and the argument ignores fundamental differences between humans and objects.

source_article: "Good morning! Have you gotten over the grouchy mood you were in?"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The speaker asks "Have you gotten over the grouchy mood you were in?" Conclusion: The listener was in a grouchy mood.
Step 2 - Pattern: The question presupposes that the listener was in a grouchy mood, showing the speaker's intent to frame the listener negatively without providing any evidence of such a mood, thereby attempting to win the conversational argument by embedding an unsubstantiated claim.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows the listener's mood is not established. The speaker still claims the listener was grouchy using an incorrect argument (a loaded question that assumes the mood).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (the question) does not provide any evidence for the listener's mood; it merely asserts it through a presupposition, making the argument intentionally unsupported.

source_article: Dean, the company you work for just filed for bankruptcy! How can I trust you with our money?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Dean works for a company that filed for bankruptcy. Conclusion: Therefore, Dean cannot be trusted with our money.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because one person (Dean) is associated with a company that failed, he personally possesses the negative attribute of untrustworthiness, generalizing from the company's situation to the individual.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically Guilt by Association). Canonical form: [MSK1] is associated with [MSK2] which has attribute [MSK3]. Therefore, [MSK1] has attribute [MSK3]. Instantiation: Dean is associated with his company which filed for bankruptcy. Therefore, Dean is untrustworthy.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the company's bankruptcy does not logically imply that Dean personally is untrustworthy; the reasoning incorrectly generalizes a corporate failure to an individual's character.

source_article: The house is white; therefore it must be big
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The house is white. Conclusion: The house must be big.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that being white is necessarily connected to being big, but no such logical or causal link is provided; the conclusion leaps from a property of the house (color) to an unrelated property (size) without any supporting premise.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Non sequitur. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true. Therefore, [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: [The house is white] is true. Therefore, [the house is big] is true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the color of a house has no logical or empirical connection to its size; a white house can be small, medium, or large, so the premise does not support the conclusion.

source_article: This seat belt ad is using which type of persuasive appeal? It has a picture which might make people feel sad.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The ad has a picture which might make people feel sad. Conclusion: The ad is using an emotional persuasive appeal (specifically appeal to pity).
Step 2 - Pattern: The reasoning identifies an emotional element (sadness) in the ad and labels it as a persuasive appeal without considering other possible appeals or evidence of manipulative intent.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: The claim that the ad uses an emotional appeal is made without evidence; in place of evidence, the presence of a sad picture is taken as proof that the appeal is emotional.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the ad is using appeal to emotion does not follow from the premise because a picture causing sadness does not necessarily mean the ad is purely emotional or manipulative; it could be using sadness to support a rational argument (e.g., highlighting risks of not wearing a seat belt).

source_article:
Mom: (looking at report card) How do you have an F in English?!
Son: I don’t know.  Did you see that loaded the dishwasher?  I was about to go mow the lawn.  Need me to do anything else?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The son mentions loading the dishwasher, mowing the lawn, and asking if there is anything else to do. Conclusion: This response is intended to address the mother’s question about the F in English.

Step 2 - Pattern: The son introduces unrelated household chores to divert attention from the mother’s argument about his failing grade, rather than explaining why he has an F.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that doing household chores implies the F in English is not a concern, whereas the chores are unrelated to the academic performance.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the son’s response addresses the mother’s point does not follow because loading the dishwasher and mowing the lawn are irrelevant to why he received an F; it merely shifts focus away from the grade issue.

source_article: A smoke screen to cover the truth; a clue that is misleading or distracting.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The text describes a smoke screen and a misleading or distracting clue. Conclusion: This is a fallacy of relevance.

Step 2 - Pattern: The text defines a red herring—an irrelevant or misleading element introduced to divert attention from the main issue—which matches the fallacy of relevance pattern.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that a smoke screen or misleading clue implies the truth is covered or hidden, whereas the smoke screen is unrelated to the actual truth.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that this describes a fallacy of relevance does not follow because the text merely defines the concept; it does not present an argument where an irrelevant premise leads to an unrelated conclusion.

source_article: President Clinton being a moral man, being married and loving his daughter; talking about the Bible; going to church
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: President Clinton is described as a moral man, married, loving his daughter, talking about the Bible, and going to church. Conclusion: President Clinton is a moral man.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument lists personal and religious attributes to imply morality, but these do not constitute actual evidence of moral behavior in the context of the argument. The speaker intends to win the argument by appealing to these traits rather than providing substantive evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: The speaker knows that listing these attributes does not prove President Clinton is moral in a specific context, yet still claims he is moral using an incorrect argument (appeal to personal and religious qualities).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (listing personal and religious traits) does not logically prove moral character; the argument intentionally uses these traits as a substitute for direct evidence.

source_article: Asking a respected scientist to weigh in on matters of economics is what fallacy?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: A respected scientist is asked to weigh in on matters of economics. Conclusion: This is a fallacy.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument implies that a scientist's authority in their own field (science) does not automatically extend to economics, suggesting that invoking their opinion on economics is an unwarranted appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] is an expert in [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK1] is an expert in [MSK3]. Instantiation: A respected scientist is an expert in science. Therefore, a respected scientist is an expert in economics.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because expertise in one domain (science) does not automatically confer expertise in another (economics); the argument mistakenly uses the scientist's credibility in an unrelated field.

source_article: We think that Malltime will be a success because all little girls like shopping.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: All little girls like shopping. Conclusion: Malltime will be a success.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because some individuals (little girls) have a certain attribute (liking shopping), the entire category (all little girls) uniformly holds that attribute, and then applies that generalization to predict the success of a specific entity (Malltime) without considering other relevant factors.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Little girls like shopping. Little girls are a subset of all people. Therefore, all people like shopping.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Malltime will be a success does not follow from the premise because it relies on an unsupported generalization that all little girls like shopping, and it ignores other factors like the specific target audience, competition, and market conditions that determine success.

source_article: Evolution is the idea that humans come from pond scum.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "Evolution is the idea that humans come from pond scum." Conclusion: Evolution is false or ridiculous.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker replaces a rational definition of evolution with an emotionally charged, oversimplified caricature to provoke disgust or ridicule, thereby avoiding factual evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion (specifically, Appeal to Ridicule). Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: The claim that evolution is false is made without evidence; instead, the statement "humans come from pond scum" is used to evoke ridicule and disgust, making the audience reject evolution emotionally.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that evolution is false or ridiculous does not follow because the premise misrepresents evolution as a crude, emotionally repulsive idea rather than providing evidence against the scientific theory.

source_article: Katie likes to read and would rather do that than play sports. Her friends make fun of her and tell her that reading is for nerds. Katie stops reading so much and starts to play sports more.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Katie's friends make fun of her and tell her that reading is for nerds. Conclusion: Katie stops reading so much and starts to play sports more.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument implies that because the majority (her friends) believe reading is for nerds, Katie should abandon reading and adopt the majority's preferred activity (sports).
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad populum. Canonical form: A lot of people believe [MSK1]. Therefore, [MSK1] must be true. Instantiation: A lot of people (Katie's friends) believe reading is for nerds. Therefore, reading is for nerds (and Katie should stop doing it).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Katie should stop reading and play sports does not follow from the premise; the popularity of the belief among her friends does not make reading inherently undesirable or wrong.

source_article: The research and reasoning that supposedly supports (or that supposedly discredits) this intervention are a joke. The researchers are people who are not methodologically sophisticated and there have been rumors--I have no idea whether they're true or not--that they faked some of the data. The advocates (or opponents) of this intervention are the worst kind of sloppy thinkers. They are fanatical adherents who already have their minds made up; they've become true believers in their cause. They make arguments only a stupid person would accept, and mistakes in reasoning that would make an undergrad psych major blush. These are not the kind of people who deserve to be taken seriously.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The researchers and advocates are methodologically unsophisticated, rumored to have faked data, sloppy thinkers, fanatical, and make arguments only a stupid person would accept. Conclusion: The research and reasoning supporting or discrediting the intervention should not be taken seriously.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the character, competence, and motives of the people making the argument (researchers and advocates) instead of addressing the actual research, reasoning, or evidence regarding the intervention.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a moron. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The researchers and advocates are claiming the intervention is supported or discredited. They are described as methodologically unsophisticated, rumored data-fakers, sloppy thinkers, fanatical, and making stupid arguments. Therefore, their research and reasoning are a joke and should not be taken seriously.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because attacking the person's character or credibility does not logically disprove or undermine the actual evidence or reasoning about the intervention; the truth of a claim is independent of the flaws of the person making it.

source_article: It occurs when someone makes an appeal to emotion of pity instead of addressing the argument.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The text describes a scenario where someone appeals to pity instead of addressing the argument. Conclusion: The text defines what an appeal to emotion is.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text itself is a definition or description of the fallacy, not an instance of the fallacy being committed. It does not manipulate emotions to win an argument.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is not a fallacy; it is a meta-commentary. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: The text does not instantiate this; it merely describes it.
Step 4 - Verdict: The text does not commit the fallacy of appeal to emotion because it is a definition, not an argument using emotion to persuade.

source_article:
Person 1: Bicycle infrastructure should be expanded because cycling is a sustainable mode of transportation. 
Person 2: We should not build bike lanes because cyclists run red lights and endanger pedestrians.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Person 1 claims bicycle infrastructure should be expanded because cycling is sustainable. Conclusion: Person 2 argues we should not build bike lanes because cyclists run red lights and endanger pedestrians.

Step 2 - Pattern: Person 2 distorts Person 1’s argument for expanding bicycle infrastructure (based on sustainability) into an attack on cyclists’ behavior (running red lights, endangering pedestrians), then uses that distorted version to reject the original proposal.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Person 1 makes claim “bicycle infrastructure should be expanded because cycling is sustainable.” Person 2 restates this as “we should not build bike lanes because cyclists run red lights and endanger pedestrians” (a distorted focus on negative behavior). Person 2 attacks the distorted version. Therefore, expanding bicycle infrastructure is implied to be wrong.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that we should not build bike lanes does not follow from the premise, because Person 2 has replaced Person 1’s sustainability-based argument with an unrelated critique of cyclist behavior, ignoring the original claim.

source_article: A commercial featuring starving children in Africa asking for donations to help provide food and clean water to those in need.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Starving children in Africa are shown in a commercial asking for donations. Conclusion: Viewers should donate to help provide food and clean water.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses a vivid emotional appeal (images of starving children) to prompt action, rather than providing evidence that donations will effectively solve the problem or that this charity is efficient.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: [The claim that one should donate] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion (pity for starving children) is used to convince viewers that [donating is the right action].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that one should donate does not logically follow from the premise because the commercial relies on emotional manipulation rather than factual reasoning about the charity's effectiveness, cost, or alternative solutions.

source_article:
J: Cats are better than dogs.
V: Dogs are better.
J: You are a lazy person. You won't even be able to take care of a dog.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: J claims cats are better than dogs. Conclusion: V is a lazy person and cannot take care of a dog.
Step 2 - Pattern: Instead of addressing V's argument that dogs are better, J attacks V's character by calling them lazy and incapable, thereby dismissing V's position.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a lazy person. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: V is claiming dogs are better. V is a lazy person who cannot take care of a dog. Therefore, dogs are not better.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because V's laziness or ability to care for a dog is irrelevant to the truth of the claim that dogs are better than cats.

source_article: 1. “iPhones are the best smartphones on the market because Apple makes the best smartphones.”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Apple makes the best smartphones. Conclusion: iPhones are the best smartphones on the market.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses the claim that Apple makes the best smartphones to support the conclusion that iPhones are the best, but the premise is essentially the same claim restated, offering no independent evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: "iPhones are the best smartphones" is true because "Apple makes the best smartphones"; "Apple makes the best smartphones" is true because "iPhones are the best smartphones."
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that iPhones are the best does not follow from the premise because the premise merely rephrases the conclusion without providing any external support or evidence.

source_article: My neighbor told me that the position of the sun, moon, and stars decide our personalities. I believe her because she is a famous violin player.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My neighbor told me that the position of the sun, moon, and stars decide our personalities. I believe her because she is a famous violin player. Conclusion: The position of the sun, moon, and stars decides our personalities.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the authority of a famous violin player, whose expertise is in music, not in astrology or personality science, making it an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] is an expert in an unrelated field. Therefore, [MSK2] should be believed. Instantiation: [MSK1] = my neighbor (famous violin player), [MSK2] = the position of the sun, moon, and stars decides our personalities.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because being a famous violin player does not provide expertise or evidence in astrology or personality science; the authority is irrelevant to the claim.

source_article:
Steve: In Sweden, college is free for citizens. How come we can't do that here?

Ed: If you like Sweden so much, move there. The USA would be glad to be rid of your liberal ass!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Steve suggests that the USA should adopt Sweden's free college system. Conclusion: Ed dismisses Steve's suggestion by attacking Steve personally.
Step 2 - Pattern: Ed does not address Steve's argument about free college; instead, he attacks Steve's character ("liberal ass") and suggests he should leave the country.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a liberal ass. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Steve is claiming that the USA should have free college like Sweden. Steve is a liberal ass. Therefore, the USA should not have free college.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Steve's argument is invalid does not follow from the premise because the attack on Steve's political label is irrelevant to the merit of his proposal about free college.

source_article:
Senator: I will vote to increase the defense budget.

Public: Why don't you care about education?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Senator says "I will vote to increase the defense budget." Conclusion: Public implies the Senator does not care about education.

Step 2 - Pattern: The Public distorts the Senator's specific policy position (voting for more defense spending) into a broad, dismissive accusation of not caring about education, then attacks that distorted accusation instead of addressing the original defense budget claim.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Senator makes claim "I will vote to increase the defense budget." Public restates this as "you don't care about education" (a distorted, unrelated accusation). Public attacks the distorted version (implying the Senator is uncaring). Therefore, the Senator's original claim is dismissed.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the Senator does not care about education does not follow from the premise that the Senator will vote to increase the defense budget, because supporting defense spending does not logically imply opposition to education; the Public has replaced the original argument with a weaker, unrelated accusation.

source_article: Delores is a big supporter for equal pay for equal work.  This is the same policy that all those extreme feminist groups support.  Extremists like Delores should not be taken seriously -- at least politically.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Delores supports equal pay for equal work. Premise: Extreme feminist groups also support this policy. Conclusion: Delores is an extremist and should not be taken seriously politically.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because Delores shares one policy with a group labeled "extreme," she herself must be an extremist, and then dismisses her entirely based on that label.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically guilt by association). Canonical form: [MSK1] shares attribute [MSK2] with group [MSK3]. [MSK3] is labeled as extreme. Therefore, [MSK1] is extreme and should be dismissed. Instantiation: Delores shares the attribute of supporting equal pay with extreme feminist groups. Those groups are labeled extreme. Therefore, Delores is extreme and should not be taken seriously.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because sharing one policy with a group does not automatically make someone a member of that group or share all its attributes. Delores may support equal pay for different reasons than extreme feminists, and the label "extremist" is not justified by this single commonality.

source_article: I've had bad luck for so long that I'm bound to have good luck now.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I've had bad luck for so long. Conclusion: I'm bound to have good luck now.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because a long sequence of bad luck has occurred, good luck must follow as a balancing effect, treating independent random events as causally connected.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred repeatedly, then [MSK2] must occur to balance it. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Bad luck] occurred repeatedly, then [good luck] must occur now. Therefore, [bad luck] caused [good luck].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because luck is typically random and independent; past bad luck does not causally determine future good luck, and the argument commits the Gambler's Fallacy, a subtype of false causality.

source_article: "Everyone who likes pizza likes pepperoni" is an example of
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The statement "Everyone who likes pizza likes pepperoni" is given as an example. Conclusion: This statement is an example of a faulty generalization.
Step 2 - Pattern: The fallacy occurs when a universal claim about all members of a group is made based on insufficient evidence, such as assuming a specific attribute (liking pepperoni) applies to every member of a broader category (people who like pizza) without proper justification.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [People who like pepperoni] has attribute [liking pepperoni]. [People who like pepperoni] is a subset of [people who like pizza]. Therefore, all [people who like pizza] has attribute [liking pepperoni].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because liking pepperoni is not a necessary condition for liking pizza; many pizza lovers may prefer other toppings or plain cheese, so the generalization is unsupported.

source_article: If you don’t go to the party, then your friends will have fun without you. If your friends have fun without you, then they will stop inviting you to fun things. If your friends stop inviting you to fun things, you will have no social life. Therefore, if you don’t go to this party, then you will have no social life.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: If you don’t go to the party, then your friends will have fun without you. If your friends have fun without you, then they will stop inviting you to fun things. If your friends stop inviting you to fun things, you will have no social life. Conclusion: Therefore, if you don’t go to this party, then you will have no social life.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument constructs a chain of hypotheticals where each step assumes a deterministic outcome, generalizing from a single party absence to a total loss of social life without considering alternative possibilities or exceptions.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically a Slippery Slope). Canonical form: [MSK1] (not going to the party) leads to [MSK2] (friends having fun without you), which leads to [MSK3] (stop inviting you), which leads to [MSK4] (no social life). Instantiation: The text asserts that one instance of missing a party will inevitably cascade into a complete loss of social life, generalizing from a single event to an extreme outcome.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because it assumes each step is necessary and without mitigating factors; missing one party does not guarantee that friends will stop inviting you entirely, nor that losing invitations leads to no social life, as there are many other social opportunities and relationships.

source_article: When your parent asks you why you have a bad grade in math class, and you answer, "All teachers are the enemy!"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The child answers, "All teachers are the enemy!" Conclusion: This answer is a relevant response to the parent's question about why the child has a bad grade.

Step 2 - Pattern: The child introduces an unrelated claim about teachers being enemies to divert attention from the parent's question about the specific reason for the bad grade, rather than addressing the actual cause (e.g., lack of studying or difficulty with the material).

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that "All teachers are the enemy" implies an explanation for the bad grade, whereas the general claim about teachers is unrelated to the specific reason for the poor performance.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that the child's response addresses the parent's question does not follow because the statement about teachers being enemies is irrelevant to explaining why the grade is bad; it merely shifts focus away from the actual cause.

source_article: "My roommate said her philosophy class was hard, and the one I'm in is hard, too. All philosophy classes must be hard!”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My roommate said her philosophy class was hard, and the one I'm in is hard, too. Conclusion: All philosophy classes must be hard.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker generalizes from only two specific instances (their roommate's class and their own class) to all philosophy classes, ignoring the possibility that other classes may differ.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: "My roommate's philosophy class" and "my philosophy class" have attribute "hard". These two classes are a subset of "all philosophy classes". Therefore, all philosophy classes have attribute "hard".
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because two examples are insufficient to prove a universal claim; other philosophy classes may vary in difficulty due to different professors, topics, or students.

source_article:
No animals are insects.
No insects are dogs.
Therefore, no dogs are animals.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: No animals are insects. Premise: No insects are dogs. Conclusion: No dogs are animals.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument uses two negative universal premises ("no A are B", "no B are C") to derive a negative universal conclusion ("no C are A"), but this is invalid because the premises only state that insects are excluded from both animals and dogs, yet they do not establish any relationship between animals and dogs; for example, animals and dogs could still overlap.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Exclusive Premises. Canonical form: Two negative premises cannot yield a valid categorical syllogism. Instantiation: Both premises are negative ("no animals are insects" and "no insects are dogs"), so no conclusion about animals and dogs follows.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because two negative premises in a categorical syllogism do not entail any positive or negative relationship between the remaining terms; animals and dogs could be the same set or disjoint, but the argument wrongly asserts they are disjoint.

source_article:
Claim: You can't give me a C in this course...
Reason: ... Because I am an A student.
Warrant: An A student is someone who can't receive Cs.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: I am an A student. Conclusion: You can't give me a C in this course.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument defines "A student" as someone who cannot receive Cs, so the premise that the speaker is an A student already assumes the conclusion that they cannot get a C, creating a loop.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: "You can't give me a C" is true because "I am an A student." "I am an A student" is true because "An A student is someone who can't receive Cs."
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise already contains the conclusion; no independent evidence is provided to support why the speaker is an A student without assuming the very claim in dispute.

source_article: an attempt to persuade the reader/audience based on feelings or emotions; emotional response
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The text describes an attempt to persuade based on feelings or emotions. Conclusion: The described action is an appeal to emotion.
Step 2 - Pattern: The text defines the fallacy by its mechanism (using emotions) rather than presenting an argument that manipulates emotions in a specific context.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is appeal to emotion. Canonical form: [MSK1] is made without evidence. In place of evidence, emotion is used to convince the interlocutor that [MSK1] is true. Instantiation: The text itself does not instantiate the fallacy but defines it; it is a meta-description.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the text is a definition, not an argument; it lacks a claim [MSK1] made without evidence and emotional manipulation.

source_article: The economy continues to grow as the number of "likes" on my Instagram account continue to increase. Clearly, the two are linked.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The economy continues to grow as the number of "likes" on my Instagram account continue to increase. Conclusion: Clearly, the two are linked.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because two events (economic growth and Instagram likes) occur together in time, one must cause the other, without any evidence of a causal mechanism or considering other factors.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [The number of "likes" on my Instagram account increased] occurred, then [the economy continued to grow] occurred. Therefore, [the increase in Instagram likes] caused [the economic growth].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because mere correlation or temporal coincidence does not prove causation; economic growth is determined by complex factors like investment, employment, and policy, not by social media likes, and no evidence is provided for a causal link.

source_article: All teachers drink coffee and teachers are people; therefore, all people drink coffee.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: All teachers drink coffee. Premise: Teachers are people. Conclusion: Therefore, all people drink coffee.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that a property (drinking coffee) that holds for a subset (teachers) automatically holds for the entire superset (all people), without justification that the subset is representative.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Teachers] has attribute [drink coffee]. [Teachers] is a subset of [people]. Therefore, all [people] have attribute [drink coffee].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise only provides evidence about teachers, not about all people; many people are not teachers and may not drink coffee.

source_article: Parents today would rather their kids suppress their identity rather than just be who they are even if it doesn't go with the norm.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: (Implied) Some parents might prefer their kids to suppress their identity rather than be who they are. Conclusion: All parents today would rather their kids suppress their identity rather than be who they are even if it doesn't go with the norm.

Step 2 - Pattern: The statement takes a specific observation or assumption about some parents and applies it universally to all parents today, without providing sufficient evidence that this behavior is widespread or representative.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (Hasty Generalization). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Some parents today have attribute "would rather their kids suppress their identity". Some parents today are a subset of parents today. Therefore, all parents today have attribute "would rather their kids suppress their identity".

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because there is no evidence that the behavior attributed to some parents is true for all parents today; the generalization is based on an insufficient or unrepresentative sample.

source_article:
Charlie: Illegal posting and sharing of songs online is crippling the music industry.

Bob: You couldn’t be more wrong; the music industry is doing just fine. I can’t believe you think the government should be allowed to regulate what I share with my “friends.” No one wants a world where I can’t loan a book to my girlfriend, let my roommate borrow my iPod, or share a funny meme with my blog followers.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Charlie claims illegal posting and sharing of songs online is crippling the music industry. Conclusion: Bob attacks Charlie as believing the government should regulate what he shares with friends, like loaning a book or sharing a meme.

Step 2 - Pattern: Bob distorts Charlie’s specific claim about illegal song sharing into a broad, extreme position that the government should control all personal sharing (books, iPods, memes), then attacks that exaggerated version instead of addressing the original point about illegal downloads.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of extension. Canonical form: [MSK1] makes claim [MSK2]. [MSK3] restates [MSK2] (in a distorted way). [MSK3] attacks the distorted version of [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] is false. Instantiation: Charlie makes claim “illegal posting and sharing of songs online is crippling the music industry.” Bob restates this as “the government should be allowed to regulate what I share with my ‘friends,’” including loaning a book or sharing a meme (a distorted extreme). Bob attacks the distorted version as wrong. Therefore, Charlie’s claim is implied to be false.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Charlie’s claim is wrong does not follow from the premise, because Charlie’s argument about illegal song sharing does not entail government regulation of personal, non-commercial sharing like loaning a book or sharing a meme; Bob has replaced the original argument with a weaker, distorted version.

source_article:
"I can't trust that doctor's advice on how to raise my child. He has never been a mother.
This is an example of
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The doctor has never been a mother. Conclusion: Therefore, his advice on raising a child cannot be trusted.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks the doctor's personal experience (lack of motherhood) instead of addressing the validity of the medical advice itself.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] lacks [some personal attribute]. Therefore, [MSK2] is not trustworthy. Instantiation: The doctor is claiming advice on raising a child. The doctor has never been a mother. Therefore, his advice is not trustworthy.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the doctor's gender or parental status is irrelevant to the accuracy or expertise of his professional medical advice.

source_article: in response to the claim that "Eating fast food is unhealthy": “But I saw you eat a burger and fries for lunch only a few hours ago!"
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Someone claims "Eating fast food is unhealthy." Conclusion: The claim is false because the person ate a burger and fries for lunch.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker attacks the person's behavior (eating fast food) instead of addressing the argument about the health effects of fast food.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] does [MSK3]. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: The person is claiming "Eating fast food is unhealthy." The person eats a burger and fries. Therefore, the claim is not true.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the person's personal eating habits are irrelevant to the truth of the claim about the health effects of fast food.

source_article: Every time I wash my car, it rains the next day. My car washing definitely affects the weather.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Every time I wash my car, it rains the next day. Conclusion: My car washing definitely affects the weather.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because one event (washing the car) consistently precedes another event (rain the next day), the first event must cause the second, ignoring the possibility of coincidence or other factors.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Washing my car] occurred, then [rain the next day] occurred. Therefore, [washing my car] caused [the rain].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because mere temporal correlation does not prove causation; the rain could be due to unrelated weather patterns, and the observed sequence may be coincidental.

source_article: Barbara believes the marketing agency's office is haunted. The cleaning crew once said they heard a other-worldly noise late at night. Since no one could prove that it wasn't a ghost, the office must be haunted.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The cleaning crew once said they heard an other-worldly noise late at night. No one could prove that it wasn't a ghost. Conclusion: The office must be haunted.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker uses the inability to disprove a ghost as positive evidence for the ghost's existence, showing an intent to win the argument by appealing to ignorance rather than providing direct evidence for haunting.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is intentional. Canonical form: [MSK1] knows [MSK2] is incorrect. [MSK1] still claims that [MSK2] is correct using an incorrect argument. Instantiation: Barbara knows that "no one could prove it wasn't a ghost" does not prove the office is haunted. She still claims the office must be haunted using an appeal to ignorance.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the premise (lack of disproof) does not logically imply the conclusion (haunting); the argument intentionally shifts the burden of proof to assume a ghost is the only remaining explanation.

source_article: Everyone loves Rebecca, because she is so popular.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Rebecca is popular. Conclusion: Everyone loves Rebecca.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that being popular (a general, vague attribute) necessarily implies that everyone loves her, without providing evidence that this popularity is universal.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (hasty generalization). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Rebecca has the attribute of being popular. Rebecca is a subset of the set of people. Therefore, all people love Rebecca.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because popularity is not equivalent to universal love; being popular only means many people like her, not everyone, and the premise lacks evidence for the universal claim.

source_article: Victor, the company you work for just filed for bankruptcy! How can I trust you with our money?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Victor works for a company that just filed for bankruptcy. Conclusion: Victor cannot be trusted with our money.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker assumes that because Victor's employer is bankrupt, Victor personally is untrustworthy, generalizing from the company's financial failure to an individual's character.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization (specifically, guilt by association). Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: Victor's company has attribute "filed for bankruptcy." Victor is a subset of the company (an employee). Therefore, Victor has attribute "untrustworthy."
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because a company's bankruptcy does not logically imply that an individual employee is personally untrustworthy with money; the attribute of the group (financial failure) is not necessarily an attribute of each member (personal integrity).

source_article: America:  love it or leave it. Either you’re for us or against us.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: There are only two possible attitudes toward America: love it or leave it; only two sides in a conflict: for us or against us. Conclusion: Therefore, these are the only options available.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument reduces a complex set of possible stances (patriotism, criticism, neutrality, etc.) to just two extreme choices, ignoring middle grounds or alternative positions.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either love America or leave it; either be for us or against us.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because there are many other possible stances, such as loving America while criticizing its policies, being neutral, or holding nuanced views, which are excluded by the false dichotomy.

source_article: “You should always do what you are being told because following the rules is important.”
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: "Following the rules is important." Conclusion: "You should always do what you are being told."
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attempts to justify a rule (doing what you are told) by appealing to the same concept (following rules) without providing independent evidence.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: "You should always do what you are being told" is true because "following the rules is important." "Following the rules is important" is true because "you should always do what you are being told."
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise merely restates the conclusion in different words, offering no new evidence or reasoning to support it.

source_article: "Avoid Latam Airlines. Their planes are always late" is what kind of fallacy?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Latam Airlines has a plane that is late. Conclusion: All Latam Airlines planes are always late.
Step 2 - Pattern: The speaker draws a universal conclusion about all flights of Latam Airlines based on a single or few instances of lateness.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [a specific late Latam flight] has attribute [being late]. [that specific flight] is a subset of [Latam Airlines flights]. Therefore, all [Latam Airlines flights] have attribute [being late].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that all Latam Airlines planes are always late does not follow from the premise of one or a few late instances; lateness can be due to many factors and does not hold for every flight.

source_article: Since many of the students at St. Cloud State University get A's, St. Cloud State must  be a top-rated school.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Many of the students at St. Cloud State University get A's. Conclusion: St. Cloud State must be a top-rated school.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that a high proportion of A grades among students is sufficient evidence to conclude the entire institution is top-rated, ignoring other factors that define a top-rated school.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is faulty generalization. Canonical form: [MSK1] has attribute [MSK2]. [MSK1] is a subset of [MSK3]. Therefore, all [MSK3] has attribute [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Students at St. Cloud State University] has attribute [getting A's]. [Students at St. Cloud State University] is a subset of [St. Cloud State University]. Therefore, [St. Cloud State University] has attribute [top-rated].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because having many students with A's does not necessarily mean the school is top-rated; a top-rated school is defined by factors like faculty quality, research output, and reputation, not just grade distribution.

source_article: He can't be a great athlete; he cheated on his wife.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: He cheated on his wife. Conclusion: He can't be a great athlete.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the person’s moral character (infidelity) instead of evaluating his athletic ability or performance.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2] or is an athlete. [MSK1] cheated on his wife. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true or he cannot be a great athlete. Instantiation: The person is claiming to be a great athlete. He cheated on his wife. Therefore, he is not a great athlete.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because personal infidelity is irrelevant to athletic skill; the argument dismisses the claim based on unrelated personal behavior.

source_article: Marco cusses a lot, so you can't listen to a thing he says. He's not a good guy.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Marco cusses a lot. He's not a good guy. Conclusion: You can't listen to a thing he says.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks Marco's character and behavior (cussing, not being a good guy) instead of addressing the content or validity of whatever he says.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem. Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] cusses a lot and is not a good guy. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: Marco is claiming something. Marco cusses a lot and is not a good guy. Therefore, what Marco says is not worth listening to.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that you cannot listen to anything Marco says does not follow from the premise about his character or behavior, because his cussing or moral standing is irrelevant to the truth or validity of his statements.

source_article: My cousin Bob works as a mechanic at Oil Changers and he says that renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, aren't going to provide any help in fixing our nation's energy crisis.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: My cousin Bob works as a mechanic at Oil Changers and he says that renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, aren't going to provide any help in fixing our nation's energy crisis. Conclusion: Renewable energy sources are not going to provide any help in fixing our nation's energy crisis.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the testimony of a single individual (cousin Bob, a mechanic) whose expertise is in automotive maintenance, not in energy policy or renewable energy technology, which is an appeal to authority, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] claims that [MSK2]. [MSK1] is an expert in a field related to [MSK2] but not directly in [MSK2]. Therefore, [MSK2] should be believed. Instantiation: [MSK1] = cousin Bob (mechanic), [MSK2] = renewable energy sources are not helpful for the energy crisis.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because Bob's expertise as a mechanic does not qualify him to make authoritative claims about renewable energy and national energy policy; his opinion is not backed by relevant evidence or expertise.

source_article:
Morris: Oh youthful spirit, you have so much to learn.  I know for a fact that there are multiple dimensions that beings occupy.
Clifton: How can you possibly *know* that for a fact?
Morris: (raises one eyebrow, stares deeply into the eyes of Clifton and says nothing)
Clifton: Wow. You convinced me!
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Morris raises an eyebrow, stares deeply, and says nothing. Conclusion: This silence and stare is sufficient to convince Clifton that Morris knows for a fact that multiple dimensions exist.

Step 2 - Pattern: Morris attempts to use a nonverbal, dramatic gesture (silence and a stare) as evidence for his claim, which is irrelevant to establishing factual knowledge; it diverts attention from the need for actual evidence or reasoning.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of relevance. Canonical form: It is claimed that [MSK1] implies [MSK2], whereas [MSK1] is unrelated to [MSK2]. Instantiation: It is claimed that raising an eyebrow and staring silently implies the fact of multiple dimensions, whereas the gesture is unrelated to proving that knowledge.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that Clifton should be convinced does not follow because a dramatic pause and stare provide no evidence for the claim about multiple dimensions; it merely shifts focus away from the demand for proof.

source_article: You need to go to the party with me, otherwise you’ll just be bored at home.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: You either go to the party with me or you’ll be bored at home. Conclusion: You need to go to the party with me.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument presents only two options – going to the party or being bored at home – ignoring other possibilities like staying home and enjoying a hobby, reading, watching a movie, or doing other activities.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false dilemma. Canonical form: Either [MSK1] or [MSK2] is true. Instantiation: Either you go to the party with me or you’ll be bored at home.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion that you need to go to the party does not follow from the premise because the premise falsely restricts the options to only two, ignoring many other ways to avoid boredom at home.

source_article: Free trade will be good for this country. The reason is patently clear. Isn't it obvious that unrestricted commercial relations will bestow on all sections of this nation the benefits which result when there is an unimpeded flow of goods between countries?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Unrestricted commercial relations will bestow benefits resulting from an unimpeded flow of goods between countries. Conclusion: Free trade will be good for this country.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument asserts that free trade is good because it involves unrestricted commercial relations and an unimpeded flow of goods, but these are essentially restatements of what free trade means, not independent evidence.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is circular reasoning. Canonical form: [MSK1] is true because of [MSK2]. [MSK2] is true because of [MSK1]. Instantiation: Free trade is good because unrestricted commercial relations bring benefits. Unrestricted commercial relations bringing benefits is true because free trade is good.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because the premise merely rephrases the conclusion without providing any independent justification; it assumes what it needs to prove.

source_article: Animals tread softly upon the earth without shoes, so all other living beings should follow suit.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Animals tread softly upon the earth without shoes. Conclusion: All other living beings should follow suit (i.e., not wear shoes).
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument appeals to the behavior of animals as a natural model to prescribe human conduct, which is an appeal to nature, a subtype of fallacy of credibility.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is fallacy of credibility. Canonical form: [MSK1] act in a certain natural way. Therefore, [MSK2] should act the same way. Instantiation: [MSK1] = animals, [MSK2] = all other living beings (including humans).
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow from the premise because animals' lack of shoes is due to biological adaptation, not a moral or practical prescription for humans, who have different needs and environments; natural behavior is not automatically normative.

source_article: The Volkswagen Beetle is an evil car because it was originally designed by Hitler’s army.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: The Volkswagen Beetle was originally designed by Hitler’s army. Conclusion: The Volkswagen Beetle is an evil car.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument attacks the car by associating it with a discredited person (Hitler) rather than addressing any inherent qualities or features of the car itself. This is a genetic fallacy, a subtype of ad hominem, where the origin or creator of something is used to dismiss its value or character.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is ad hominem (genetic fallacy). Canonical form: [MSK1] is claiming [MSK2]. [MSK1] is associated with [MSK3]. Therefore, [MSK2] is not true. Instantiation: [The Volkswagen Beetle] is claiming [to be a car]. [The Volkswagen Beetle] is associated with [Hitler’s army]. Therefore, [the Volkswagen Beetle] is an evil car.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the car’s moral status is not determined by its origin or designer; the argument fails to provide evidence about the car’s actual design, function, or impact, instead relying on an irrelevant attack on its historical association.

source_article: Recognising that Amanda had committed a fallacy in arguing that we should eat healthy food because a nutritionist said it was popular, Alyse said we should therefore eat bacon double cheeseburgers every day.
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Amanda argued that we should eat healthy food because a nutritionist said it was popular. Premise: Alyse responded that we should therefore eat bacon double cheeseburgers every day. Conclusion: The conclusion is that because Amanda committed a fallacy, her conclusion is false and the opposite (eat bacon double cheeseburgers) is true.

Step 2 - Pattern: The argument mistakenly assumes that if an argument contains a fallacy, its conclusion must be false, and then leaps to an extreme opposite conclusion without any supporting evidence or logical connection.

Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is Argument From Fallacy. Canonical form: If [MSK1] contains a fallacy, then [MSK1]'s conclusion is false. [MSK1] contains a fallacy. Therefore, [MSK1]'s conclusion is false. Instantiation: If Amanda's argument contains a fallacy, then her conclusion (eat healthy food) is false. Amanda's argument contains a fallacy. Therefore, her conclusion is false, and the opposite (eat bacon double cheeseburgers) is true.

Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because a fallacious argument does not automatically make its conclusion false; the conclusion could still be true for other reasons. Moreover, even if Amanda's conclusion were false, that does not justify Alyse's extreme opposite conclusion without further evidence.

source_article: Valencia bought a different brand of detergent at the store. She developed a rash after wearing clothes washed in the new detergent. She concluded that she was allergic to the new soap. What type of reasoning is this?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Valencia bought a different brand of detergent at the store. She developed a rash after wearing clothes washed in the new detergent. Conclusion: She concluded that she was allergic to the new soap.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument assumes that because one event (using the new detergent) preceded another event (developing a rash), the first event caused the second, without considering other possible causes for the rash.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is false causality. Canonical form: [MSK1] occurred, then [MSK2] occurred. Therefore, [MSK1] caused [MSK2]. Instantiation: [Valencia bought and used a different brand of detergent] occurred, then [she developed a rash] occurred. Therefore, [the new detergent] caused [her allergic reaction].
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the rash could be due to other factors such as a pre-existing allergy, contact with another irritant, or a different cause, and mere temporal succession does not prove causation.

source_article: surgeons have X-rays to guide them during an operation, lawyers have briefs to guide them during a trial, carpenters have blueprints to guide them when they are building a house. Why, then, shouldn’t students be allowed to look at their textbooks during an examination?
reasoning:
Step 1 - Structure: Premise: Surgeons have X-rays to guide them during an operation. Premise: Lawyers have briefs to guide them during a trial. Premise: Carpenters have blueprints to guide them when building a house. Conclusion: Students should be allowed to look at their textbooks during an examination.
Step 2 - Pattern: The argument draws an analogy between professionals using reference materials in their work and students using textbooks during exams, but it ignores the key difference that exams are meant to test knowledge without external aids, while professional tasks are not.
Step 3 - Label + Canonical Form: This is False Analogy. Canonical form: [A] and [B] are similar in some respects. [A] has property [P]. Therefore, [B] has property [P]. Instantiation: Surgeons, lawyers, and carpenters use reference materials in their professional tasks. They are allowed to use those materials. Therefore, students should be allowed to use reference materials in examinations.
Step 4 - Verdict: The conclusion does not follow because the analogy is flawed: examinations are designed to assess individual knowledge and recall, whereas the professional contexts involve ongoing tasks that require guidance, not memory testing.
